Complexity: 3

Authors: @sandy081


[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323332%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

This covers two related improvements for multi-chat agent host sessions: choosing the **agent and model per chat**, and surfacing **progress and file changes at the session level** across peer chats. Go through the flows and report anything confusing or wrong.

## Set up

- Open a **Copilot session in the Agent Host** (this is a multi-chat session).

## Flows to try

1. **Create a session with a custom agent**
   - Create a new session and select a **plugin/extension-contributed custom agent** (for example a custom agent contributed in your workspace), then send a message.
   - It should start and respond normally ΓÇö no "Custom agent 'ΓÇª' not found" error.

2. **Per-chat agent & model**
   - Create more than one chat in the session. In each chat, change the **agent and the model** independently (using the pickers in the chat input).
   - Each chat should keep its own agent/model choice.

3. **Aggregated progress across chats**
   - Start work in a non-active peer chat (so another chat is the focused one).
   - While that chat is running: the **session status** should show as in-progress (not Idle), and **each chat tab** that is working should show its own progress ΓÇö not just the first/main chat.

4. **Aggregated changes (Changes pill)**
   - Have one or more peer chats make file edits.
   - The session header **Changes pill** should appear and, when opened, show the **aggregated changes** from the peer chats (not only the main chat's edits).

> Note anything where the agent/model didn't stick per chat, a working chat didn't show progress, or changes were missing.
input.title: Test: Per-chat agent/model selection, aggregated progress & changes

input.body:
Refs: #322844

- [x] anyOS @aiday-mar
- [x] anyOS @digitarald

Complexity: 2

Authors: @sandy081


[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323330%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

This test is about the **multi-chat experience** in an agent host session ΓÇö creating, closing, reopening and deleting chats. Go through the flows below as a real user would. The goal is not just "does it work", but **does the UX make sense** ΓÇö is it discoverable, predictable, and does it feel right? Please file issues for anything confusing, surprising, or awkward.

## Set up

- Open a **Copilot session in the Agent Host** (this is a multi-chat session).

## Flows to try

1. **Creating chats**
   - Use the **`+ New Chat` button in the session header** (next to the session controls) to create a chat. Once more than one chat is open, a **chat tab strip** appears with its own trailing **`+`** ΓÇö use that to add more. Continue real conversations in each.
   - Did the way you create a new chat feel obvious and stay consistent as the number of chats grew? Did switching between tabs feel natural?

2. **Closing and reopening**
   - Close a chat using the **close (x) icon on its tab**. Then reopen it from the **Conversations dropdown**. Note where the dropdown lives: while there is a single open chat (tab strip hidden) it sits in the **session header**; once the **chat tab strip is shown** (more than one open chat) it moves to the **right-aligned end of the chat tab bar**. Each chat is listed with a checkbox ΓÇö uncheck hides it, check shows it.
   - Could you tell that closing **hides** a chat rather than losing it? Was the Conversations menu easy to find in both placements? Did moving it to the end of the tab bar (when tabs are shown) feel natural, or did you expect it somewhere else?

3. **Deleting**
   - Permanently delete a chat via **right-click on its tab ΓåÆ Delete Chat**.
   - Was the difference between **close** (x on the tab) and **delete** (context menu) clear? Did the destructive action feel appropriately distinct/safe? Were you ever unsure whether a chat was gone for good or just hidden?

4. **End-to-end sanity**
   - After creating/closing/reopening/deleting, switch away and back to the session (or reload).
   - Did everything stay in the state you expected?

> Throughout: note anything that was confusing, took more than one try to find, or didn't match your expectations ΓÇö that feedback is the point of this TPI.
input.title: Test: Create, close, reopen & delete chats + New Chat UX in the agents window

input.body:
Refs: #322799

- [x] anyOS @vritant24
- [x] anyOS @hediet

Complexity: 4

Authors: @sandy081


[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323329%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

Forking a conversation in an **agent host session that supports multiple chats** now creates a **new peer chat in the same session** (instead of a brand-new top-level session). Single-chat and non-agent-host sessions keep the existing fork-into-a-new-session behavior.

This is an **exploratory** test ΓÇö try forking from different points and conversation states.

## Set up

- Open an **agent host copilot session that supports multiple chats**
- Have a conversation with at least a few back-and-forth turns so there is something to fork.

## Scenarios

1. **Fork creates a peer chat (multi-chat session)**
   - From a multi-chat agent host session, fork the conversation (fork action on the conversation / a selected request).
   - Expected: a **new chat appears in the same session** alongside its siblings ΓÇö not a new top-level session.
   - The new chat should contain the inherited conversation up to (but not including) the request you forked from.

2. **Fork boundary**
   - Fork from a specific earlier request.
   - Expected: the new chat keeps everything **before** that request (the selected turn is the last one kept), matching the new-session fork behavior.

3. **Forked chat title**
   - After forking, the new chat should get an auto-generated title derived from the inherited conversation (it should read like a normal title, not literally say "forked"/"branched").
   - If you start a manual `/rename` right after forking, your rename should win (auto-title is suppressed).

4. **Forked chat is independent**
   - Continue the conversation in the forked chat.
   - Expected: it runs separately and does not affect the source chat or its siblings.

5. **Single-chat session keeps old behavior**
   - Open a **Claude Code session in the agent host** (single-chat ΓÇö does not support multiple chats) and fork the conversation.
   - Expected: the existing behavior ΓÇö fork opens a **new session** (not a peer chat).

6. **Exploratory**
   - Try forking an empty/very short chat, forking repeatedly, forking the forked chat, and forking while another chat is running. Note any odd titles, missing turns, or focus issues.
input.title: Test: Fork a chat in a multi-chat agent host session

input.body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes - VS Code Version: 1.126.0 - Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a - OS Version: Windows NT 10.0.26200.0 ## Description The Outline view of a Jupyter notebook displays Markdown cells containing LaTeX display equations as outline entries, even when **Markdown Headers Only** is enabled and **Code Cells** and **Code Cell Symbols** are disabled. The equations are incorrectly treated as section headings. This makes the Outline very noisy and interferes with hierarchical section folding. The issue persists after: - Reloading the VS Code window. - Disabling all installed extensions. - Confirming that `notebook.outline.showMarkdownHeadersOnly` is enabled. - Checking that the notebook cells contain no unusual metadata. The same notebook hierarchy is displayed correctly in Google Colab. ## Steps to Reproduce 1. Create or open a Jupyter notebook. 2. Add a Markdown cell containing: ```markdown # Section Some explanatory text. $$ x^2 + y^2 = 1 $$ The LaTeX equation appears as an additional item in the Outline, for example: Section ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ $$ x^2 + y^2 = 1 $$ In larger notebooks, many equations are incorrectly displayed as nested outline entries. Expected Behavior Only actual Markdown headings beginning with #, ##, ###, etc. should appear in the Outline when Markdown Headers Only is enabled. The equation cell should not appear as an outline entry.
input.title: Notebook Outline shows LaTeX display equations despite ΓÇ£Markdown Headers OnlyΓÇ¥

input.body: Refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323317 - [x] macOS @federicobrancasi - [x] Windows @rzhao271 Complexity: 3 [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323317%0A%0A&assignees=vijayupadya) [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323318%0A%0A&assignees=vijayupadya) --- ## Context There is now a /troubleshoot slash command/skill that can be used to debug **agent host** sessions. Prerequisite: - Ensure you have couple of local agent host sessions already completed - Ensure you have couple of remote agent host sessions already completed (for info on creating remote sessions see https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agents/remote-agent-sessions) ## Local Agent Host Test Steps 1. Create a new chat session for troubleshooting, select 'Local agent host Copilot CLI' from drop down 2. Type /troubleshoot slash command and then type #session, then you should see your previous _local_ sessions show up in the UI (only local should show up and not remote) as shown in image below, select one of them. Then type in your question for troubleshooting (you can leave it blank in which case it will explain what happened in the session) You may have to approve few operations for reading the log file Few example scenarios for troubleshooting: - Why is model not showing up? - Why did my instruction/skill/agent file not used? - Why did the request take so long? - Why did a tool call fail? - Explain what happened in this session - Why did you not follow my instructions ### Verification - Verify that troubleshoot cmd shows up in the autocomplete - Verify that as you typed #session only local sessions shows up in the UI for selection and Pressing TAB or ENTER selects that session and adds it to the chat context - Verify that the response from troubleshoot is 'satisfactory', response was for the session you selected and is helping you with the scenario you are trying to troubleshoot. ## Remote Agent Host Test Steps Prerequisite (same as above): - Ensure you have couple of local agent host sessions already completed - Ensure you have couple of remote agent host sessions already completed (for info on creating remote sessions see https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agents/remote-agent-sessions) 1. Create a new chat session for troubleshooting, select 'Remote' and connect to same remote that you created remote sessions earlier 2. Type /troubleshoot slash command and then type #session, then you should see only remote sessions show up in the UI. Then type in your question for troubleshooting (you can leave it blank in which case it will explain what happened in the session) ### Verification - Verify that troubleshoot cmd shows up in the autocomplete - Verify that as you typed #session only remote sessions shows up in the UI for selection and Pressing TAB or ENTER selects that session and adds it to the chat context - Verify that the response from troubleshoot is 'satisfactory' and it was for the session you selected and is helping you with the scenario you are trying to troubleshoot.
input.title: Test: /troubleshoot skill for agentHost sessions

input.body: Refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/299521 - [x] anyOS @connor4312 - [x] anyOS @benvillalobos Complexity: 5 [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323315%0A%0A&assignees=kycutler) --- The integrated browser now supports a handful of site-level permissions: - Location - Camera - Microphone - Sensors - Clipboard - Notifications - Devices ## Setup You can use https://thetest.com for most of the testing. Simply open it in an integrated browser tab. > [!IMPORTANT] > On MacOS, you may have to enable some permissions for Code - Insiders in system settings. If some scenarios (e.g. location or notifications) don't work, double check that the MacOS permission is enabled too. > [!NOTE] > When in doubt about some behavior, compare it against Chrome or Edge. ## Scenarios ### Permission defaults and requests Open the "Permissions" test and verify: - Camera, Microphone, Notifications, and Geolocation all show "Denied" by default. - Hardware Sensors should show "Granted" by default, and Clipboard should show "Prompt". - Clicking on any of the denied ones (or "Prompt") opens a dialog with the site name and allow / deny controls. - Choosing "Allow" or "Block" behave appropriately. Choosing "Cancel" will result in a timeout after 30s. ### Permissions can be managed for the current site Under the overflow menu, select "Site Permissions": Use the controls in the dialog to change the selection. Note that Sensors and Devices are allowed by default but can be explicitly blocked. Select the Save button to persist the changes. ### Permission-gated APIs behave as expected Open each of the related tests: - "Camera" - "Microphone" - "Public IP / Location" - "Sensors" - Note: most laptops don't have motion sensors so you likely won't see much here except battery percentage. - "Connected Devices" - You only need to check the bottom 4: "WebHID", "WebUSB", "Web Bluetooth", and "Web Serial". Just ensure that they show a dialog to choose a device and you can either choose one or Esc to cancel. Ensure that each behave as expected. #### Notifications The notifications test on this site doesn't seem to work (even in a regular browser). You can ask chat to generate an html file for testing notifications, or use the DevTools to run `new Notification('testing')` and confirm it appears. #### Clipboard There also isn't a test on this site for clipboard, so use https://clipboardify.com/. You should be able to "Copy" without giving permission, but "Paste" should require allowing the clipboard permission. ### Permissions are origin-specific Open any other site that requests some permissions. Ensure that its permissions are managed independently and each site persists its own settings. You can also just open the "Site Permissions" dialog to help test this. ### Files have independent permissions Open an HTML file and do similar testing. You can ask chat to implement some tests as well. Ensure that each file maintains its own set of permissions. ### Permissions are cleared with storage Ensure that the "Clear Storage (__)" command clears all site permissions. If you don't want to delete all of your saved browser data you can first set `workbench.browser.dataStorage` to `ephemeral` or `workspace`, open a new tab and test there, and then run the "Clear Storage ([Ephemeral | Workspace])" command.
input.title: Test: browser site permissions

input.body:
## Problem

Client-provided tool calls running in a session's **default chat** can hang: the SDK handler's deferred is never resolved, so the agent waits forever on a tool result that already came back.

## Root cause

`ChatToolCallComplete` envelopes are emitted on the chat channel URI, which is formatted as:

```
ahp-chat://<chatId>/<base64(sessionUri)>
```

In `agentSideEffects.ts`, both dispatch paths forwarded that chat URI straight into `IAgent.onClientToolCallComplete(session, ...)`. But every agent keys its `_sessions` map by the raw session id, resolved via `AgentSession.id(uri)` = `uri.path.substring(1)`. For a chat URI that path is the **base64 blob**, not the session GUID, so the default-chat lookup never matched and the completion was silently dropped.

This affected the copilot, claude, and codex agents. Peer (additional, non-default) chats happened to still work because they resolve through a separate `_chatSessions` map keyed by the chat URI.

## Fix

Forward the resolved session URI for the default chat (so `AgentSession.id` yields the GUID), and keep forwarding the chat URI for peer chats. Tracked in [#323302](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323302).

## Follow-ups to consider

- The footgun is that `onClientToolCallComplete(session, ...)` accepts a URI that is sometimes a session URI and sometimes a chat URI, and each agent re-derives routing. Worth considering a clearer contract or a single shared resolution helper so this can't silently regress.
- Add broader coverage that the URI handed to agents at the side-effects boundary is always the one the agent can resolve.
input.title: Agent host: client tool calls in the default chat hang (never resolved)

input.body:
## Type of Bug

Bug

## Description

Restoring an Agent Host Copilot session can split one SDK interaction into multiple AHP turns and therefore multiple `ChatResponseViewModel` instances. The additional requests have empty prompts.

The Copilot SDK can persist empty `assistant.message` events with no tool requests immediately before another assistant message in the same `interactionId` and SDK `turnId`. The live adapter ignores these non-renderable messages, but `mapSessionEvents` treats every parent assistant message without tool requests as the end of the current turn. The following assistant message then has no active builder, so history replay creates a fallback turn with an empty user message.

The resulting chain is:

1. `mapSessionEvents` prematurely finalizes the parent turn.
2. The next assistant message creates a fallback turn with an empty prompt.
3. `turnsToHistory` emits a request/response pair for every reconstructed turn.
4. `ChatServiceImpl` and `ChatViewModel` create multiple top-level response view models.

Captured restored models showed six empty requests created within the same millisecond. Their request IDs exactly matched the orphaned SDK `assistant.message` event IDs.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a tool-heavy Copilot Agent Host conversation that produces multiple SDK model rounds.
2. Switch away from the session or restart/reopen it so history is rebuilt from SDK events.
3. Inspect the chat model or rendered transcript.
4. Observe multiple requests/responses with empty prompts for what was one live interaction.

## Expected Behavior

All assistant messages belonging to the same SDK interaction are reconstructed into one AHP turn and one top-level chat response, preserving markdown/tool ordering. Empty, non-renderable assistant events must not create a turn boundary.

## Relevant Code

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/mapSessionEvents.ts`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/test/node/mapSessionEvents.test.ts`

(Written by Copilot)
input.title: Copilot session restore splits one interaction into multiple chat responses

input.body:
## Bug Report

### Description

When switching the Copilot Chat mode from **Agent** to **Plan** mid-conversation, VS Code automatically injects a synthetic `"Start implementation"` user message into the chat and begins executing ΓÇö without any input from the user.

This is unexpected behaviour: the user explicitly switched to Plan mode expecting a planning/review workflow, not for the agent to silently fire off a request and begin modifying files.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open Copilot Chat in **Agent** mode.
2. Have an existing conversation (e.g. discuss a feature or task).
3. Switch the mode selector from **Agent** ΓåÆ **Plan** (without typing anything new).
4. Observe: a `"Start implementation"` message is automatically injected as a user message, and the agent begins working.

### Expected Behaviour

Switching modes should be a passive operation. It should **not** send any message or trigger any action. The user must explicitly type a new message to interact after switching modes.

### Actual Behaviour

The mode switch silently fires `"Start implementation"` as a user request, which causes the agent to begin implementing changes immediately ΓÇö bypassing the user's intention to enter planning/review mode.

### Impact

- Users lose control of the agent mid-session.
- Files can be modified without explicit user intent.
- The feature is unusable if you want to review/plan without triggering execution.

### Environment

- VS Code version: latest (2026-06-27)
- OS: Linux
- Extension: `GitHub.copilot-chat`

### Workaround

Start a **new chat session** and select Plan mode before typing anything. Do not switch modes mid-conversation.
input.title: Copilot Chat: switching from Agent to Plan mode mid-conversation auto-injects \"Start implementation\" and begins executing

input.body:
## Summary

For every MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin available in GitHub Copilot Chat, **clearly display the VS Code extension that introduced it** ΓÇö visible both from the Customizations view and from the installed extension itself.

## Problem

These objects are registered from many places, often **silently** when you install a VS Code extension. They land globally in the user folder (e.g. `~/.agents/skills`) or inside extension folders (`agents`, `prompts`, etc.).

Today there is **no way to know which VS Code extension introduced a given MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, or plugin.** When one of them causes unexpected behavior, tracing it back to the responsible extension means manually digging through folders and guessing ΓÇö it's a nightmare.

## Proposed Solution

Make the **originating VS Code extension explicitly visible**, from both directions:

- **From the Customizations view** ΓåÆ show, next to each MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin, the extension that created it (e.g. an "Extension" / "Source" column or on hover).
- **From the installed extension** ΓåÆ show a summary of the objects it created (the MCP servers, tools, agents, skills, instructions, and plugins it registers).
- For items not coming from an extension, label them as user or workspace config instead.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin shows the VS Code extension that introduced it in the Customizations view.
- Each installed extension shows a summary list of the objects it created.
- Items not provided by an extension are labeled as user/workspace config.
- Optional: quick action to open/reveal the underlying file on disk.

## Benefits

- Instantly identify which extension added any given object ΓÇö and what each extension adds.
- Far easier debugging and cleanup.
- Transparency over what extensions register behind the scenes.
input.title: AI: Show the VS Code extension that introduced each MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin

input.body:
## Summary

Give developers a simple on/off toggle for every **MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin** individually, directly inside the existing **Customizations** view in GitHub Copilot Chat for VS Code.

## Problem

The model context is assembled from many sources, and **installing VS Code extensions silently adds more of them** ΓÇö extra agents, skills, tools, instructions, and plugins get registered without the developer asking for them or being able to easily turn them off. The result:

- Context bloat from items you never opted into.
- Unexpected behavior from a skill or instruction you didn't know was active.
- Hard to debug which MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, or plugin caused a bad response.
- Wasted tokens on things you don't use.

## Proposed Solution

The Customizations view already lists these items. Add an **on/off toggle** next to each individual entry, plus a group-level toggle per category:

- MCP servers
- Tools
- Agents
- Skills
- Instructions
- Plugins

No new screen ΓÇö reuse the existing Customizations UI.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin has its own visible enable/disable toggle; each category has a master toggle.
- Disabling an item removes it from the context immediately (no restart).
- Toggle state is persisted (per workspace, with user/global option).
- Toggling only deactivates an item ΓÇö it never deletes the underlying config.

## Benefits

- Full control over what extensions inject into Copilot's context.
- Easier debugging, less bloat, lower token usage.
input.title: AI Chat: Add on/off toggles in the Customizations view to enable/disable each MCP server, tool, agent, skill, instruction, and plugin

Version: 1.126.0
Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a
Date: 2026-06-23T01:46:13Z
Electron: 42.2.0
ElectronBuildId: 14159160
Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
Node.js: 24.15.0
V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
OS: Darwin arm64 25.5.0

**_Prefixed the issue with "Critical:", because if this affects popular frontier model usage, the consequences of leaving this bug unattended are not great._** I sincerely hope this bug is limited to local model usage only.

I cannot verify backend behavior on hosted providers such as OpenAI or Claude, because their server-side processing is not visible to the user. To make cancellation behavior observable, I reproduced the issue against a locally hosted model on my Mac using LM Studio, where server-side prompt processing and token generation can be directly observed. 

Here's a video (sorry it's 4:30 mins long)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/189ff252-3ab9-4e04-ba28-72f378e05e98

**The problem:**

Pressing **Stop** in built-in **VS Code Copilot Chat** cleanly stops the conversation at the UI level, but does **not** reliably abort the backend inference request. The model continues prompt processing and token generation on the LM Studio server, long after the user has cancelled in the VS Code chat UI.

I verified this with a controlled comparison against the **same LM Studio backend**, on the **same machine**:
- **Built-in VS Code Copilot Chat**: pressing **Stop** leaves the backend request running and tokens continue generating server-side. Interrupting and chatting with the agent multiple times leads to multiple long-running token generation processes whose outputs will never reach the user. 
- **Cline extension in VS Code**: pressing **Stop** correctly aborts backend processing immediately.

This strongly suggests the problem is in the **VS Code Copilot Chat cancellation/abort path**, not in LM Studio itself.

## Impact

- Wastes CPU/GPU and battery locally.
- Causes significant heat/fan usage on laptop hardware, even when not using chat.
- Misleads the user because the IDE appears to have stopped the request while the backend is still running.
- Potentially serious under usage-based billing or token-metered backends, because a user may believe generation has stopped when backend token generation may still be ongoing.

## Repro steps

1. Start LM Studio local server and load a model.
2. Configure built-in VS Code Copilot Chat to use the LM Studio-backed local model.
3. Send a prompt that takes noticeable prompt-processing or generation time.
4. While the request is still running, press **Stop** in VS Code chat.
5. Observe LM Studio server logs or UI:
   - prompt processing and/or token generation continues after **Stop** was pressed. 
6. Repeat the same test with **Cline**, an alternative to VS Code chat, against the same LM Studio backend.
7. Observe that Cline stops backend generation correctly, leaving no wasteful orphans.

## Expected result

Pressing **Stop** in VS Code Copilot Chat should propagate cancellation to the backend request and terminate prompt processing or token generation immediately.

## Actual result

The chat UI stops, but the backend request may continue running as an orphaned inference job. 

## Evidence

I recorded a **4 minute 30 second comparison video** showing:
- built-in VS Code Copilot Chat behavior
- Cline behavior
- LM Studio server-side processing visible during both tests

The recording clearly shows that with built-in VS Code chat, backend processing continues after **Stop**.
If you interrupt the agent with stopping and messaging many times, this leads to a bunch of long-lived "orphan" inferences whose results never reach the user.

With Cline, the inference gets stopped immediately every time.

## Environment

- LM Studio version: Version 0.4.17+4 (0.4.17+4)


## Notes

This issue may be easy to dismiss as a backend/runtime problem, but the comparison test against **Cline using the same LM Studio backend** strongly suggests the backend is capable of proper cancellation and that the failure is specific to the built-in Copilot Chat integration path. 

Currently, this makes VS Code chat with local models unusable for me. I sincerely hope this doesn't affect premium requests with copilot subscriptions.
input.title: Critical: Built-in VS Code chat "Stop" button silently fails to abort backend LLM inference, leaving orphaned token generation running

Bonjour,

Concernant l'accessibilit├⌐ num├⌐rique, VS Code n'est pas fiable lorsqu'il est utilis├⌐ avec VoiceOver. J'ai rencontr├⌐ plusieurs probl├¿mes en l'utilisant.

* Le texte situ├⌐ sous le curseur VoiceOver ne correspond pas au texte affich├⌐ sur l'afficheur braille.
* Lorsque j'arrive en bas d'une page, le curseur VoiceOver reste bloqu├⌐.
* Dans la barre lat├⌐rale qui contient les fichiers et les dossiers, je ne peux pas lire leurs noms sur mon afficheur braille.
* Dans le terminal (ou la console), je ne peux pas lire correctement les messages d'erreur. Parfois rien ne s'affiche, et parfois je ne vois que ┬½ ? ┬╗ ou ┬½ group ┬╗.
* Il existe ├⌐galement plusieurs dysfonctionnements lors des suggestions de code et de fonctions.

Si je devais r├⌐sumer mon exp├⌐rience avec VS Code et VoiceOver sur macOS en une seule phrase :

**Il est tr├¿s difficile de travailler efficacement ├á cause d'importants probl├¿mes de synchronisation entre le curseur VoiceOver et l'afficheur braille.**

Ces probl├¿mes sont d├⌐j├á connus. J'ai trouv├⌐ plusieurs discussions ├á leur sujet sur Internet.

Pour information, macOS est largement utilis├⌐ en entreprise et constitue le syst├¿me d'exploitation privil├⌐gi├⌐ par de nombreuses personnes d├⌐ficientes visuelles.

Il serait vraiment dommage que l'accessibilit├⌐ de la version macOS de VS Code ne soit pas am├⌐lior├⌐e.

Cordialement,

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Extensions can create integrated terminals with `vscode.window.createTerminal`, and can choose locations such as the panel, editor area, or a split beside a parent terminal via `location: { parentTerminal }`.

However, there does not appear to be a public API for creating a new terminal adjacent to a related existing terminal while keeping the terminal UI as a flat tab list.

For example, an extension command may create a new terminal using the current working directory of the active terminal. If the active terminal is named `infra`, the new terminal is logically related to `infra`, so it would be useful for the new terminal to appear immediately after `infra` in the terminal tab list.

Currently the extension can either:

- create a normal terminal with the desired `cwd`, but VS Code decides the tab order
- create a split terminal with `location: { parentTerminal }`, but that changes the UI layout into a split pane

Neither option preserves a flat terminal tab list while keeping related terminals grouped together.

## Related issues

I found these related issues before filing this:

- #253020 requested broader terminal tab reordering APIs and was closed as out of scope.
- #309068 requested move terminal tab up/down commands and was closed after not reaching the upvote threshold.

This request is intentionally narrower than a general-purpose terminal reordering API. It focuses on creation-time placement for a newly created terminal, specifically adjacent to a parent/source terminal.

## Describe the solution you'd like

Add a way for extensions to create a terminal next to an existing terminal without creating a split pane.

One possible API shape:

```ts
const terminal = vscode.window.createTerminal({
  cwd,
  location: vscode.TerminalLocation.Panel,
  insertAfter: parentTerminal,
});
```

Another possible API shape could extend terminal location options:

```ts
const terminal = vscode.window.createTerminal({
  cwd,
  location: {
    parentTerminal,
    split: false,
    placement: "after",
  },
});
```

The exact API shape is not important; the important part is allowing extensions to keep a new terminal adjacent to a related terminal without forcing a split layout.

## Describe alternatives you've considered

- `location: { parentTerminal }`: creates a split, which is useful for some workflows but not for users who want a flat terminal tab list.
- Creating the terminal normally and naming it after the source terminal: helps identify related terminals, but does not keep them grouped in the UI.
- Manual drag-and-drop after creation: works interactively, but cannot support an extension command that consistently preserves workflow grouping.

## Additional context

This would help extensions that create workflow-specific terminals from an existing terminal context, such as:

- creating another terminal in the active terminal's current working directory
- opening task-specific terminals next to their source terminal
- grouping terminals by project or service without changing the terminal panel layout into splits

The goal is not full terminal layout management, only a small creation-time placement hint for related terminals.
input.title: Allow extensions to create terminals adjacent to a parent terminal without splitting

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

<!--
IMPORTANT: Please search existing issues before creating a new one.
This issue explicitly focuses on the display/rendering of relative numbers on wrapped lines, NOT cursor movement bugs or line numbering on physical lines.
-->

### Problem Description

When using `editor.lineNumbers: "relative"` combined with `editor.wordWrap: "on"`, VS Code only renders relative line numbers in the gutter for **physical lines** (lines ending with a `\n` character). 

The virtual lines generated by the soft wrap do not display any line numbers in the gutter. They are completely blank. 

This layout makes it impossible for users navigating with Vim-like extensions (or using advanced keyboard-driven workflows via `cursorMove` with `{ by: "wrappedLine" }`) to know the exact relative distance to a specific virtual line. The user has to manually count the wrapped lines on the screen to perform a precise vertical jump (e.g., `5j`), which completely defeats the purpose of relative line numbers.

This request specifically targets the capability to **dynamically display relative numbers directly on virtual lines in the gutter**.

### Proposed Solution

Introduce a new configuration option or expand `editor.lineNumbers` to support virtual lines rendering. 

For example, a new setting like:
```json
"editor.renderRelativeNumbersOnWrappedLines": true
```

#### Expected Visual Behavior:

If physical line 100 takes 3 visual lines on screen due to word wrap, and the cursor is currently on physical line 101, the gutter should display the distance to every single visual block, like so:

```text
[Gutter]   | [Code Editor View]
   4       | Physical Line 99
   3       | Physical Line 100 (Wrapped line block 1)
   2       | Physical Line 100 (Wrapped line block 2)
   1       | Physical Line 100 (Wrapped line block 3)
 [101]     | Cursor is here on Physical Line 101
```
*(Note: Whether it increments per virtual line or counts the virtual offset from the cursor line, the goal is to have a visual indicator in the gutter for every visible line on the screen).*

### Additional Context

- This is a highly requested feature for keyboard-centric workflows and modal editing.
- Currently, extensions cannot implement this as a workaround because the VS Code Decoration API and Gutter API only bind to physical line indices in the text model, preventing any rendering on soft-wrapped lines. This must be implemented natively within the Monaco editor layout engine. AFAIK.
input.title: Option to display relative line numbers for virtual/wrapped lines

input.body:
## What I want

Today an extension can set a terminal tab's **icon** and **color** only at the moment
the terminal is created (`window.createTerminal({ iconPath, color })`). After that, there
is no way for an extension to change them.

I want a way for an extension to **update the icon and color of an existing terminal tab
while it is open**, without closing and re-creating it.

## Why this matters

People now run **many** terminals at once, often long-running AI/agent or task sessions.
When you have 8 terminals open, the tab names all look similar and it is hard to tell, at
a glance, which one needs attention.

A live tab icon and color would fix this instantly:

- **Red icon** = this session needs you (your turn).
- **Yellow icon** = this session is busy working.
- **Green icon** = this session finished.

Right now I can do this in the tab **name** (text), but text is easy to miss when many
tabs are crowded together. A colored **icon** is far easier to spot. The user can already
do this by hand (right-click the tab, "Change Color" / "Change Icon"), so the feature
clearly exists. There is just no way for an extension to do it automatically.

## What works today and what does not

```js
// Works: icon and color are set once, at creation time.
const term = vscode.window.createTerminal({
  name: 'My session',
  iconPath: new vscode.ThemeIcon('play'),
  color: new vscode.ThemeColor('terminal.ansiYellow'),
});

// Does NOT work: there is no way to change them later.
// term.iconPath = new vscode.ThemeIcon('bell');         // no setter
// term.color = new vscode.ThemeColor('terminal.ansiRed'); // no setter
```

## Suggested change

I think this could be small, because VS Code already does the work internally. The
right-click menu calls `TerminalInstance.changeColor(color)` and `changeIcon(icon)`, and
those methods already set the value live when you pass one in. The only missing piece is a
way for an extension to call that with a value.

Two possible options (either one would work for me):

1. **A command that takes an argument** (my preferred option, because it copies an
   existing pattern). VS Code already has
   `workbench.action.terminal.renameWithArg` for renaming a tab from an extension. Please
   add the same style of command for icon and color, for example:
   - `workbench.action.terminal.changeColorWithArg` taking `{ color: 'terminal.ansiRed' }`
   - `workbench.action.terminal.changeIconWithArg` taking `{ icon: 'bell' }`

2. **Make the API properties writable**, so an extension can do
   `terminal.color = new ThemeColor(...)` and `terminal.iconPath = new ThemeIcon(...)`
   on an existing terminal.

## Related issues

- microsoft/vscode#167643 - related but different. That one is about the icon/color set
  **at creation time** not applying correctly (a bug). This request is about changing the
  icon/color **after** the terminal is already open (a new capability).
- microsoft/vscode#176087 - closed as a duplicate of #167643.

Thanks! Happy to help test or contribute a PR if this is something you would accept.
input.title: Let extensions change a terminal tab's icon and color after it is created

input.body:
Not links are detected as links in math, for example:
```markdown
$$
c = 3 \left[ a - b \right]
$$
```
produces a warning: `No link definition found: 'a - b \right' (link.no-such-reference)`. Obviously there is no link in the expression.
Often square brackets are used to simplify the perception of a formula with a lot of parentheses, it's not me being weird.

Since VSCode does render math, I think the issue is relevant to VSCode.

I guess links shouldn't be validated in math code at all, or only inside `\text{...}`.

input.body:
## Summary

While VS Code (Insiders) was effectively idle, the renderer consumed high CPU for several minutes. Log analysis points to a create/dispose thrash loop in the **GitHub Pull Requests** PR-icon decoration, driven by frequent re-resolution of the Chat Sessions list. A secondary, earlier spike comes from a session-refresh storm in the Agent Host (git subprocess + SQLite fan-out over all sessions).

Logs analyzed: `Agent Host.log`, `Window.log` (window/renderer), ~25 min span (16:24ΓÇô16:49), with the user-reported high-CPU window being the last ~4 min (16:45ΓÇô16:49).

## Root cause #1 ΓÇö PR-icon model thrash (renderer, the "idle" CPU)

In the last 4 minutes, `Window.log` is dominated by:

```
[PR-ICON-TRACE][GitHubPullRequestModelReferenceCollection][createReferencedObject] Creating PR model for microsoft/vscode/319368
[PR-ICON-TRACE][GitHubPullRequestModelReferenceCollection][destroyReferencedObject] Disposing PR model for microsoft/vscode/319368
... (create immediately followed by dispose, repeated)
```

- **476 PR-model create + ~476 dispose** cycles in 4 minutes ΓÇö for only **7 unique PRs** (`microsoft/vscode/319368, 317395, 313630, 311984` + `microsoft/agentrc/141, 76, 74`).
- Trigger: `[ChatSessionsService] Resolved 162 sessions for provider copilotcli` fires **12ΓÇô26├ù/min**. Each refresh re-decorates every session's PR icon by **acquiring then immediately releasing** a reference to its PR model. Refcount hits 0 ΓåÆ model disposed ΓåÆ recreated on the next pass. Each burst = exactly 14 create+dispose (7 PRs ├ù 2).
- The `PollingContribution` meant to prevent this logs `has no PR identity yet ΓÇª waiting` and never holds a warm reference, so nothing keeps the models alive between passes.

Net: thousands of full PR-model lifecycles (event wiring, Octokit setup) with **zero state change** ΓÇö pure wasted CPU. This is the dominant cost during the idle window.

**Suspected fix area:** `GitHubPullRequestModelReferenceCollection` / `PollingContribution` should hold a stable reference for tracked sessions instead of acquire-and-release per refresh, and/or `ChatSessionsService` resolution should be throttled/deduped so it doesn't re-resolve 162 sessions every few seconds.

## Root cause #2 ΓÇö session-refresh storm (Agent Host, earlier spike 16:27ΓÇô16:32)

A burst (not steady state) in `Agent Host.log`:

- `[SessionDataService] Opening database` **1182├ù** across **118 distinct `session.db`** files ΓÇö repeatedly re-opening every session's SQLite DB.
- `agentHostGitService` ran **~1,100 git commands/minute** (~18 subprocess spawns/sec): `git status -b`, `git remote -v`, `git symbolic-ref`, `git diff --raw`, `git rev-parse` ΓÇö multiple per session ├ù 118 sessions.
- `git symbolic-ref refs/remotes/origin/HEAD` **fails 122├ù** (`fatal: ref ΓÇª is not a symbolic ref`) and keeps being retried ΓÇö wasted spawns.
- `[AgentHostStateManager] Action for unknown session` **181├ù** in one minute ΓÇö actions dispatched against sessions not in state (churn/desync).

This is a fan-out-over-all-118-sessions refresh rather than user-initiated work; git process spawning is the expensive part.

## Lower-impact noise
- `ProtocolServer Broadcasting` `changeset/statusChanged` (728) + `contentChanged` (510) + `session/*Changed` ΓÇö high broadcast volume tied to the refresh storm.
- `AgentService Evicting idle session` 123├ù and SDK `session.remote_steerable_changed` trace spam ΓÇö cosmetic logging.

## Bottom line
The idle high CPU is **#1**: the Chat Sessions list (162 `copilotcli` sessions) re-resolving every few seconds and forcing the GitHub PR extension to create+dispose PR models on every pass because the warm-keeping poller never engages. **#2** is a secondary startup-time git/DB fan-out storm in the agent host.

## Environment
- VS Code Insiders, macOS (Darwin)
- ~162 chat sessions across `copilotcli`/`local`/`copilot-cloud-agent` providers; 118 agent session DBs on disk
input.title: High idle CPU: PR-icon model thrash in Chat Sessions list (create/dispose churn)

input.body:
## Summary

A `read_page` (Read Page) browser tool call gets stuck forever in **"Evaluating"** when the model invokes it with empty arguments (omitting the required `pageId`). The tool crashes synchronously inside `prepareToolInvocation`, which leaves the agent-host permission request unresolved and hangs the entire turn.

## Root cause

`createBrowserPageLink` in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/browserView/electron-browser/tools/browserToolHelpers.ts` calls `pageId.toString()` without guarding against `undefined`:

```ts
export function createBrowserPageLink(pageId: string | URI): string {
	if (typeof pageId === 'string') { pageId = BrowserViewUri.forId(pageId); }
	return `[${BrowserEditorInput.DEFAULT_LABEL}](${pageId.toString()}?vscodeLinkType=browser)`; // throws when undefined
}
```

`ReadBrowserTool.prepareToolInvocation` calls `createBrowserPageLink(_context.parameters.pageId)`. When the model calls `readPage` with `{}`, `pageId` is `undefined` and `prepareToolInvocation` throws `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString')`.

Note that `ReadBrowserTool.invoke` already guards with `if (!params.pageId)` and returns a graceful error ΓÇö but `prepareToolInvocation` runs first and crashes before `invoke` is ever reached.

This is not specific to `read_page`: nearly every browser tool (`navigate`, `click`, `type`, `hover`, `drag`, `handleDialog`, ΓÇª) calls `createBrowserPageLink(params.pageId)` in `prepareToolInvocation`, so all share the same fragility.

## Evidence (from agent host logs)

1. `events.jsonl`: `tool.execution_start ΓÇª toolName: 'readPage', arguments: {}` ΓÇö required `pageId` omitted.
2. `Window.log`:
   - `[AgentHost] Invoking client tool: readPage (callId=toolu_01FgXΓÇª)`
   - `[LanguageModelToolsService#invokeTool] Error from tool read_page with parameters {}: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString'): TypeError` (stack: `createBrowserPageLink` ΓåÆ `prepareToolInvocation`)
   - `[AgentHost] Client tool rejected pre-execution: readPage Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString')`
3. `Agent Host.log`: `permission.requested ΓÇª readPage` (custom-tool) is emitted, but unlike the sibling `bash` permission request ΓÇö which receives `Permission response: approved=true` followed by `permission.completed` ΓÇö the `readPage` request has **no `permission.completed`** and **no `tool.execution_complete`**. With the permission never resolved, the UI stays in "Evaluating" indefinitely.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open a browser page in the integrated browser.
2. Have the agent call the `read_page` tool without a `pageId` argument (empty `{}`).
3. The Read Page tool call hangs in "Evaluating" and never completes.

## Fix

Make `createBrowserPageLink` resilient to a missing/empty `pageId` ΓÇö return a plain label instead of throwing, so `prepareToolInvocation` succeeds and `invoke` returns its proper "No page ID provided" error to the model instead of hanging the turn.

## Related (separate, agent-host runtime ΓÇö not this repo)

A pre-execution client-tool rejection should still emit `permission.completed` / `tool.execution_complete` so that a crashing client tool can never leave the turn stuck. Worth tracking separately in the Copilot CLI runtime.
input.title: Browser `read_page` tool hangs the turn ("Evaluating") when called without required `pageId`

Refs: Issue https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321478 PRs https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322585 https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323152

- [x] anyOS @jukasper 
- [x] anyOS @benibenj 

Complexity: 5


[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323222%0A%0A&assignees=jruales)

---

### Prerequisites
Make sure that these settings are enabled (They should already be, but just in case):
```
"chat.agentHost.enabled": true,
"workbench.browser.enableChatTools": true,
"workbench.browser.agentHostChatToolsEnabled": true,
```

### What to verify
**The key thing to test:** The Integrated Browser agentic experience should work just as well with Local Agent Host as it did with the previous, Local harness.

Here's a suggested way to verify this:
1. Open a copy of **VS Code - Insiders** and a copy of **VS Code (Stable)**.
    * Insiders will be used to verify the behavior with Local Agent Host (New). Stable will be used to compare with what Local was like (Old)
2. Find a front-end project that you would like to work on, or create a new one from scratch.
3. Copy that project so you have two identical folders with the same starting state. Open one of them in VS Code Insiders, and the other in VS Code (Stable)
4. **Important:** Set the agent harness type: **Copilot** in VS Code Insiders, **Local** in VS Code (Stable)
5. Set the same LLM model in both harnesses
6. Ask Copilot to do things that require the browser
7. The behavior should be similar or almost identical in Agent Host and in Local. If there's any difference, the Agent Host one (the one in Insiders) should feel like the better one, and not the other way around.

### Some specific areas that need attention
The following are some areas that work well in Local, but where **Agent Host** had difficulties in the past that would be worth checking to make sure they work well in Agent Host:
* Is the agent aware of the browser tools? (See this recent issue that was fixed this milestone https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321478. See also this old TPI: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/297090)
* Is the agent aware of shared browser tabs? (See also this other old TPI https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/297103)
* Is the agent aware of browser tabs that are open but not shared?
* https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/313808
* https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/313813
input.title: Test: Browser Tools + Agent Host

input.body:
Summary:
When opening a session in the Agents window the header shows there are changes, but the Changes view (right side) and the changes modal show no diffs. This appears intermittent and may be a race condition when a session is being restored.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Have VS Code open.
2. Open the session in the Agents window.
3. Search for a session that was open in VS Code and open it in Agents.
4. Observe the changes indicator in the header at the top showing there are changes.
5. The Changes view (right side) shows nothing.
6. Click the changes in the header to open the modal ΓÇö the modal shows no changes.

Expected:
The Changes view and the changes modal should display the diffs corresponding to the header indicator.

Actual:
No changes are shown in the Changes view or modal despite the header indicating changes.

Additional details / context:
- This is intermittent and not reliably reproducible. I suspect a race condition when a session is being restored.
- This was discussed in Slack and is related to an earlier issue where changes are missing: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/320243
- There are logs attached to the related issue, but recent logs may help reproduce ΓÇö previous logs were a bit stale.

Please investigate potential race conditions during session restore that could cause the Agents window to fail to populate the changes view/modal.


[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782496034381059?thread_ts=1782496034.381059&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)
input.title: Agents window: Changes view/modal shows no diffs when opening a session (possible race during session restore)

input.body:
The GitHub app wants to link directly from a session in the app to a session in Chat in VS Code.

This does not work today. VS Code can open the right workspace, but the user must manually open Chat and click into the chat from the app (which does automatically show).

To fix this, we need a way for other apps to deep-link into VS Code to 1. open a workspace 2. open Chat 3. Open a specific chat.
input.title: Support deep links into a specific chat

input.body:
## Problem

Chat markdown rendering currently treats both `~text~` and `~~text~~` as strikethrough because Marked's GFM tokenizer accepts one or two tilde delimiters. This can accidentally strike out text when an agent uses unrelated single tildes.

The Markdown preview uses the stricter double-tilde syntax, so the two VS Code markdown surfaces behave inconsistently.

## Expected behavior

- `~text~` renders literally.
- `~~text~~` renders as strikethrough.
- Other GFM rendering remains unchanged.

(Written by Copilot)
input.title: Chat: Require double tildes for strikethrough rendering

input.body:
Feedback from accessibility sync:

In the current UI model, where editors and browser tabs are mixed together, it's difficult to quickly switch between the "edit" and "verify" flows compared to an external browser where you can easily go back and forth with e.g. `Alt+Tab`.

Potential solutions:
- Add a setting to always open browser tabs in a particular location -- e.g. the current editor group (same as today), a dedicated side group (locked from other editors), or a new window.
- (Preferred) have a dedicated panel for browsers, like terminal does today. Then have a shortcut akin to `` Ctrl+` `` to quickly toggle that panel.
input.title: Make it easier to switch between browsers and editors

input.body:
## Feature request

When a tree item is revealed ΓÇö either programmatically via the extension `TreeView.reveal()` API, or by built-in auto-reveal in the Explorer / Source Control views ΓÇö the item is scrolled to the **top edge** of the viewport, with no surrounding rows visible. You lose all sense of *where* the item sits among its siblings (the files immediately above/below it).

The underlying list/tree widget already supports revealing an element at a **fractional vertical position** internally (the tree's `reveal(element, relativeTop)` / list `reveal` plumbing). That capability just isn't surfaced anywhere a consumer can reach it:

1. **Extension API gap** ΓÇö `TreeView.reveal(element, { select, focus, expand })` has no option to control scroll alignment. An extension that reveals a node cannot ask for it to be centered or shown with surrounding context; it always lands pinned to the top.
2. **No user setting** ΓÇö Explorer / SCM auto-reveal always pins the revealed item to the top edge. `explorer.autoReveal` is only `true | false | "focusNoScroll"`, and `focusNoScroll` *suppresses* scrolling, which is the opposite of wanting context.

## Proposal

1. Add an optional alignment to the `TreeView.reveal` options, e.g. `reveal(element, { select, focus, expand, alignment?: 'top' | 'center' })` (or a numeric `relativeTop`), surfacing the existing internal capability to extensions.
2. Optionally, a user setting for built-in auto-reveal alignment, e.g. `explorer.revealAlignment` / `scm.revealAlignment`: `"top" | "center"`.

## Why this isn't a duplicate

Earlier discussions (#63406, and related #23902 / #65549) led to *removing* always-centered reveal in favour of top-pinning. This request isn't asking to flip the default back ΓÇö it asks to make alignment **configurable / API-exposed** so both preferences are served, rather than hard-coding one. The "reveal with context" behaviour matters for review workflows where an extension steps through items and reveals each one.

## Use case

I maintain a git-diff-review extension that reveals the file currently under review in the SCM/Explorer tree as the user steps through changes. Because reveal pins to the top edge, the reviewed file ends up flush against the top with no visible context. An alignment option (or a `relativeTop`) would let the extension reveal it with a few rows of breathing room above and below.
input.title: Expose reveal scroll-alignment to the TreeView API (and an Explorer/SCM "reveal with context" option)

input.body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Extensions are required ``` Version: 1.126.0 (user setup) Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a Date: 2026-06-23T01:46:13Z Electron: 42.2.0 ElectronBuildId: 14159160 Chromium: 148.0.7778.97 Node.js: 24.15.0 V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 ``` Steps to Reproduce: Reference https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-cpptools/issues/14491 1. Using the C++ extension, use the source below ``` C++ class test {}; namespace fw { template Tv &GetTrackedActivePgnSignal(Tv &, test &, int); } class RotAngle_GoodValue_CommRx {}; class RotAngle_GoodValue_Comm {}; struct RotAngle_GoodValue_CommRx : RotAngle_GoodValue_Comm { friend RotAngle_GoodValue_CommRx &fw::GetTrackedActivePgnSignal(RotAngle_GoodValue_CommRx &, test &, int); }; ``` Notice how the last letter 'x' seems to be its own token, but the 'Developer: Inspect Editor Tokens and Scopes' dialog indicates there are two overlapping semantic scopes and that should not be possible. It actually appears that there might be three overlapping regions here, as the color doesn't match what the dialog indicates. I looked into the Semantic Token API and it appears that it is not supposed to allow overlapping regions, but it would seem that it is not checking for partial overlaps. Obviously, this is being caused by an extension that is not operating correctly, but it would probably help the extension authors find issues like this if the API produced some kind of exception when this occurs.
input.title: Semantic Token API seems to accept overlapping token ranges causing rendering weirdness

input.body: Refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/322564 - [x] anyOS @Yoyokrazy - [x] anyOS @alexr00 Complexity: 1 [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323189%0A%0A&assignees=rwoll) --- ## Context In Untrusted Worksapces, certain features are inactive or restricted. For copilot chat, you should not be able to successfully have chat respond to you. Instead, when you send a message it should trigger the Workspace Trust Dialog: Previously, the Model Picker: would show an incomplete/stale/misleading list of models in an Untrusted Workspace since certain model loading code was not active (by design). This was confusing and sometimes folks concluded their sign in or extension was broken. To mitigate this, we have updated the Model Picker's behavior in Untrusted Workspaces to be well defined. It should now show as: ΓÇªand if you click Trust Workspace, it'll trigger the same dialog/modal that sending an initial message triggers. This also applies to _inline_ chat too: ## Steps 1. Create a new directory in a location that's not trusted by your VS Code setup. 2. Open that new directory in vscode. Observe the Workspace Trust Banner on the top of the window. 3. Open the chat panel and ensure the Model Picker says "Pick Model" (not auto or some other model), and that if you expand it and click Trust Workspace, it triggers the dialog above. 4. Repeat the above but toggel the harness (Local/Claude/Copilot/etc.) and ensure the behavior is consistent. Next test Inline Chat: 1. Create a new file in the workspace, then trigger inline chat and confirm the model picker in there also has the same treatment. Finally, test Agents Window: 1. Launch the Agents Window. 2. Open a conversation you already have worked with. 3. Open the command pallette > Manage Workspace Trust > and switch the folder to Untrusted. 4. Confirm the Model Picker in your conversation now has the Trust Workspace treatment. 5. Go to a different conversation in a different, trusted folder, and ensure that the Model Picker is unaffacted. Other scenarios to explore: * BYOK * Being signed in vs. not
input.title: Test Plan: Model Picker in Untrusted Workspaces

input.body: refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/322821, https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321707 - [x] anyOS @osortega - [x] anyOS @alexr00 - [x] anyOS @zhichli Complexity: 4 Author: @lramos15, @pwang347 [Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323186%0A%0A&assignees=cwebster-99,lramos15,pwang347) --- **Context:** We've landed a number of changes and fixes spanning the **context-size widget/picker**, **reasoning effort picker**. This meta test plan exercises both areas end-to-end and their persistence/reload behavior to ensure accuracy and expected behavior. **Relevant UI for context size** 1. Model configuration picker This should never default to long context (e.g., 1M) if you never touched it. 2. Chat context usage widget This should never default to long context (e.g., 1M) if you never touched it. The numerator reflects current usage, while the denominator will always be the active selection. You can test this by changing models and configurations and observing the denominator change before you even send a request. There should always be a "reserved" output area based on the size of the max output size. This rendered value is clamped to the context maximum (max input + max response of current selection). 3. Chat debug view logs You can find the max prompt/input size and max output size here (sum to get denominator for context usage widget). Make sure that the three numbers here never disagree (note, the denominator in the chat context usage widget is sum of input + output, e.g. 400K for GPT 5.5 while the input max is 272K. But you should never see 272K in input with denominator 1M). Also note that prompt/input being 900K is often displayed as 1M. ## Test Cases **IMPORTANT** - **Your goal is to break this - stray away from happy paths and try reloading the window in partial states, clicking on old sessions, using agents vs. editor window, use different models etc.** - Here is the expected resolution order for model configs: 1. Model config from old session (with window reload and without) 2. Model config associated with harness + location 3. Model config from last selection (this is chatLanguageModels.json, also used by certain extensions) 4. Default based on model config defaults Generally, the model config should resolve exactly the same as the model. So if the last selected model is resolved differently from the config then we also have an issue here. - Tip: consider sending in chat a message like 'Say "Opus 4.8 High 1M"' so you can easily find later what the expected value should be. ### 1. Context size picker ΓÇö default values are correct - Launch VS Code with `--transient`. - Select the Local Agent, choose a model with both a default and a long-context option (e.g. GPT 5.5). - Verify the picker defaults to the **smaller** context value (not 1M). - Send a message; the context widget shows the correct value. - Open chat debug logs and confirm `maxPromptTokens` matches the selected context (e.g. 272000). - Actually hit that amount in context (e.g., "Use the read_file tool to read 100 files in my repository. Do not ask questions or stop until you have done so.") and verify that compaction works as expected. ### 2. Restoring configs from old conversations Note: This is important because if we do not continue from the same model configuration we can end up breaking cache, which will cost more. - Select Local agent and select GPT 5.5 High with 272k context. - Start or continue a Copilot Chat session. - Restart VS Code. - Continue the existing Copilot Chat session. - Check the model/context shown in the Copilot UI. - Actually hit that amount in context (e.g., "Use the read_file tool to read 100 files in my repository. Do not ask questions or stop until you have done so.") and verify that compaction works as expected. ### 3. Restoring configs from old conversations, with modified harness+location default - Launch VS Code with `--transient`. - Select Local agent and select GPT 5.5 - Create conversation A using high effort + 1M - Create conversation B using low effort + 200K - Reload window - Click into conversation A, it doesn't show high effort + 1M Repeat the above without transient option and without reloading, and also with different harnesses. ### 4. Harness/location scoped cache - Start a conversation using one harness and change config for it - Select a new harness and change config for the same model - Go back to the first harness and verify that the original config is restored - Go to the new harness and verify that the new config is restored ### 5. Global model config - Modify the chat picker for any model - Verify that the value in `chatLanguageModels.json` (accessible from the Manage Language Models command and then the file icon at the top of the UI) is always the latest set value for the model that was modified
input.title: Test Plan: Model config behaviour

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
## Feature request

Please add support for VS Code Copilot agents to natively parse PDF files.

## Problem

Many engineering and scientific projects depend on vendor manuals, datasheets, and safety documents that are distributed as PDFs. Currently, users must extract the text from PDFs and save it to a `.txt` or `.md` file, but plain text extraction loses important information.

- Tables are flattened, reordered, or lose headers
- Images, figures, wiring diagrams, and plots are completely lost
- Captions may be separated from the associated figure
- Page numbers and section references can be lost
- Extracted text lose structure and may create incorrect context for the agent

It's difficult to trust that Copilot agents have the correct context when they can only read PDF content if it has been converted to text.

## Desired behavior

When a PDF is present in a VS Code workspace, Copilot agents should be able to natively parse and understand it without using VS Code file tools.

PDF support should handle both scanned/image-based PDFs and vector/text-based PDFs.
input.title: Allow Copilot agents to natively parse PDF files in the workspace

input.body:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.x (current, June 2026)
- VS Code Version: 1.127.x
- OS Version: Windows 11
- Feature: agent mode ΓÇö memory tool (auto-write behavior)
- Selected model: Claude (Opus/Sonnet class)
- Logs: see measured evidence below

### Shared context (this issue has a sibling: #323167)
Both this issue and #323167 stem from the memory feature operating silently/automatically. The auto-write behavior described here continually grows /memories/ files, and the corruption bug in #323167 then multiplies that content ΓÇö so the two compound each other (silent context bloat + data corruption).

### Core defect
In agent mode the model auto-persists **feature-specific and already-referenceable knowledge** into **always-loaded global memory** (`/memories/`). The first ~200 lines of user/global memory are injected into context on **every turn, in every workspace**, indefinitely. This is the wrong storage tier for this kind of content, for three reasons:

1. **Duplication of referenceable knowledge.** Feature details that already live in retrievable `docs/` files (or repo memory) get copied into always-loaded memory. The same information that a one-time on-demand `read_file` could supply *only when relevant* is instead injected into context on every turn forever. Same data, loaded continuously instead of on access ΓÇö negative value.

2. **No relevance scoping.** Global memory loads in every workspace regardless of topic. A partner servicing N customers ends up loading, in customer X's sessions, customer Y's feature notes. Even within a single app, knowledge for feature A is loaded while working on unrelated feature B.

3. **Unbounded time cost.** A feature you don't touch for 3ΓÇô4 years is still loaded on every turn in the interim, with zero corresponding use, and nothing ages it out.

### The principle being violated
Always-loaded persistent memory should hold only **small, cross-cutting, non-referenceable** facts (durable preferences, hard-won gotchas). Anything that is **(a) already retrievable from a doc, or (b) feature/workspace-specific** should NOT be auto-written to global memory ΓÇö it should stay in `docs/` (or at most repo-scoped, on-demand memory) and be pulled only when relevant. The current `<memoryInstructions>` ("record what you learned") pushes the agent to do the opposite.

### Reproduced cross-workspace leak (captured this session)
I have a multi-root setup with separate workspaces. One workspace contains **only** a Business Central (AL) app and has **no knowledge of, and no files from, a separate React Native mobile app**. From that BC-only workspace I asked the agent ΓÇö explicitly forbidding it from searching or reading any files ΓÇö what it knew about the mobile app's PO-extraction work.

It answered with mobile/Python-pipeline feature specifics (`operating_pressure_psi` as a per-`part_item` schema field, the Phase A/B/C structure, a "mobile applier" that reads `shipping_resolved.agent_code/service_code` and falls back to `carrierHint`/`serviceLevelHint` on `csr_confirm`) and **explicitly attributed all of it to an auto-loaded global memory note**, `PO-Extract-Phase2-Handoff.md`. Verbatim from its reply:

> "Where the knowledge came from: Entirely from my persistent user-memory file ΓÇª specifically the auto-loaded note `PO-Extraction-Phase2-Handoff.md` (under user memory). Nothing here came from reading the workspace, `app/docs/`, or any `.ts`/`.tsx` file in this session."

Notably it also correctly declined to fabricate the parts the note didn't contain ΓÇö so the attribution is credible, not a hallucination. This is a concrete demonstration that feature-specific notes from one workspace are loaded into an unrelated workspace's context, with zero relevance to the task at hand.

### Measured evidence (real machine, single user)
- User/global memory: 26,438 bytes Γëê 6,610 tokens, auto-loaded every turn, every workspace
- Much of it is feature-specific content that already exists in this repo's `docs/` (e.g. PO-extraction internals) ΓÇö i.e. duplicated, not unique
- All memory across all workspaces: 24 files, 156,069 bytes Γëê 39,017 tokens
- Example of mis-tiered content: workspace-specific feature notes (`phys-inv-*`, `PO-Extract-*`) auto-written to globally-loaded memory rather than left in retrievable docs

### Cost / impact (corrected ΓÇö important)
The primary cost on a **GitHub Copilot subscription is NOT direct dollars.** Copilot meters by **premium request, not tokens** ΓÇö one prompt in agent mode = one premium request ├ù the model's multiplier, with tool calls and autonomous steps excluded, and overage billed per request ($0.04/request). Token volume inside a request does not change the billed amount. So on Copilot the real cost of this bloat is:

- **Context-window dilution and answer-quality degradation** ΓÇö every turn spends a fixed slice of the window on irrelevant, often duplicated, cross-workspace notes.
- **Compounding with #323167** ΓÇö corrupted/duplicated memory enlarges that always-loaded slice further.

The dollar cost only materializes under **per-token billing (Anthropic API / BYOK)**. As an order-of-magnitude reference there: ~6,610 tokens ├ù ~420 sessions/month Γëê ~2.8M input tokens/month; at an Opus-class list rate of roughly $15 / 1M input tokens that is on the order of ~$40/month ΓÇö but this does NOT apply to a standard Copilot subscription, where the same usage is $0 at the margin.

### Requests
1. **Stop auto-persisting referenceable/feature-specific content to always-loaded memory.** Prefer leaving it in `docs/` (or on-demand repo memory) and reading it only when relevant. Reserve global memory for small, cross-cutting facts.
2. **Relevance-scope what is loaded** ΓÇö don't inject every workspace's notes into every other workspace's context.
3. **Age out / cap** stale entries, and surface the memory store's size and context footprint in the UI.
4. Allow `copilot-instructions.md` / project config to suppress or constrain memory writes (ref #308669).
5. (Secondary) make global memory writes opt-in or per-write confirmable.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Use agent mode normally over several sessions with the memory tool available, in a multi-root / multi-workspace setup.
2. Observe that feature-specific notes ΓÇö content that already exists in retrievable `docs/` ΓÇö are auto-written to `/memories/` without an explicit request to "save this."
3. From an unrelated workspace, ask the agent (forbidding file reads) about a feature that only exists in another workspace; it will recite it from auto-loaded global memory and attribute it to that note.

### Related
- #323167 ΓÇö memory file corruption (recursive self-duplication) that enlarges this always-loaded slice
- #308669 ΓÇö `<memoryInstructions>` cannot be overridden by copilot-instructions.md
input.title: Agent auto-persists referenceable/feature-specific knowledge into always-loaded global memory, inflating per-turn context cost

input.body:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.x (current, June 2026)
- VS Code Version: 1.127.x
- OS Version: Windows 11
- Feature: agent mode ΓÇö memory tool (str_replace / insert)
- Selected model: Claude Opus 4.8
- Logs: see measured evidence below

### Shared context (this issue has a sibling)
Both this issue and a companion issue stem from the memory feature operating silently/automatically. The auto-write behavior continually grows /memories/ files, and this corruption bug then multiplies that content ΓÇö so the two compound each other (silent data loss + recurring token cost on every read).

### Summary
The memory tool can corrupt a memory file by recursively re-injecting the entire file body into itself. A series of `str_replace` / `insert` edits on a single `/memories/repo/` file caused the file's H1 header block and intro paragraph to be duplicated ~15 times, nested inside unrelated later sections, growing the file from ~3 KB to ~42 KB. The duplicated content is not a clean append ΓÇö it is interleaved into the middle of existing sections, so the file is no longer coherent.

### Measured evidence (one real corrupted file)
- File: `repo/phys-inv-terminology.md`
- Size: 42,185 bytes / 720 lines (should be ~3 KB / ~60 lines)
- The H1 `# Physical Inventory ΓÇö terminology (DO NOT re-derive)` appears 15 times (should be 1)
- The "one physical shelf" intro block appears 15 times, recursively nested inside unrelated later sections
- ~10,546 tokens per read; unique payload Γëê 700 tokens ΓåÆ ~93% duplicated garbage
- The corrupted file is auto-loaded / read back on subsequent turns, so the bloat is paid repeatedly

### Relationship to #322625
#322625 ("Memory str_replace tool silently drops large newString values") notes our exact symptom as a related observation ΓÇö "mid-file content duplication (a header block was injected into an unrelated section)." This issue is a concrete, quantified instance with a reproducible artifact. The two may share a root cause at the JSON-RPC serialization boundary for large `newString` values (~4ΓÇô6 KB+).

### Impact
- Silent data corruption of user memory files
- Recurring token cost: every read of the corrupted file pays ~10.5K tokens for ~700 tokens of real content
- The user cannot easily tell a file is corrupted without manually inspecting byte size / line count

### Steps to reproduce
1. Have a `/memories/repo/` (or `/memories/`) file longer than ~150ΓÇô200 lines.
2. Issue several `str_replace` and/or `insert` edits whose `newString` is a multi-line block containing backticks / tables / fenced code, roughly 4ΓÇô6 KB each.
3. After a few edits, `view` the file: the H1 / intro block has been re-injected multiple times, nested inside unrelated sections, and the file size has ballooned.

### Attachment
The full corrupted 42 KB `phys-inv-terminology.md` is available to attach for inspection.
input.title: Memory tool corrupts files via recursive self-duplication (str_replace/insert re-injects the entire file body many times)

input.body:
Python auto-activation experience with agent host is broken. This means agent would have hard time (or additional times) before making sure to running Python scripts or code or test in a proper user desired python environment.

We used to be  able to activate via shell integration script, which the built-in shell tools from copilot cli would not run. 

Perhaps investigate activation via profile script modification and see if the child process spawn would source from zshrc, etc. 

Relevant repo for profile script modification: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-python-environments
input.title: Make sure Python auto activation work with agent host

<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
When resolving a large number of merge conflicts, opening files one by one to launch the Merge Editor becomes tedious. Currently, if multiple files with merge conflicts are selected in the Source Control view, there is no option to batch-open them into Merge Editor tabs.


I would like the ability to multi-select files under the "Merge Changes" section in the Source Control panel, right-click, and see an option like "Open in Merge Editor" (or have the default double-click/Enter key open the Merge Editor for all selected files).


This would significantly speed up the workflow for developers handling complex rebases or merges with dozens of conflicting files, allowing them to queue up their workspace quickly.
input.title: Allow opening the Merge Editor for multiple selected files simultaneously

input.body:
### Bug

Several places in the Sessions feature access `workspace.folders[0].gitRepository` without optional chaining. The workspace type is `readonly folders: ISessionFolder[]` with no non-empty guarantee, so when a session has a workspace but an empty `folders` array, `folders[0]` is `undefined` and `.gitRepository` throws a `TypeError`. This can crash the Changes view, the session header, and the changeset resolvers.

### Affected locations

- `src/vs/sessions/contrib/changes/browser/changesViewService.ts:86`
- `src/vs/sessions/contrib/providers/copilotChatSessions/browser/copilotChatSessionsChangesets.ts` (lines 30, 60, 190, 242)

Elsewhere in `src/vs/sessions/` the convention is null-safe access (`folders[0]?.`), even guarded explicitly with `workspace.folders.length > 0` in `sessionsActions.ts`. These 5 spots diverge from that convention.

### Fix

I have already made the change locally (adding optional chaining to match the existing convention) and it is ready to submit as a PR.
input.title: Sessions: folders[0].gitRepository access throws TypeError when a session workspace has no folders
