input.body: # Feature request: allow per-option descriptions in snippet choice placeholders (`${1|...|}`) ## Summary When a snippet uses a **choice placeholder** (`${1|a,b,c|}`), the dropdown shows only the raw option values. There is no way to attach an explanatory **description/label** to each option. I would like each choice option to be able to carry a short description that is rendered **at the end of the line**, the same way a snippet's own `description` is shown in the IntelliSense suggestion widget when you start typing the snippet. ## Current behavior A choice option is plain text only. The grammar defines it as: ``` choice ::= '${' int '|' text (',' text)* '|}' ``` So when the choice dropdown opens, the user just sees the bare values with nothing to explain what each one means. Example snippet: ```json "Admonition": { "prefix": "adm", "body": [ "${1|!!!,???,???+|} ${2|note,tip,warning,danger|}", " $0" ], "description": "Admonition block" } ``` When the snippet itself is suggested, IntelliSense already shows a description at the end of the line ("Admonition"): But when the **choice placeholder** dropdown opens, the options `!!!`, `???`, `???+` have no description at all - the user cannot tell what each marker does: ## Desired behavior Allow each choice option to declare a description that the dropdown renders **at the end of the line**, mirroring the snippet-suggestion widget. For the example above the dropdown could read: ``` !!! static (always shown) ??? collapsible, starts closed ???+ collapsible, starts open ``` This is purely additive: existing snippets without descriptions keep working unchanged. ## Possible syntax - Inline separator per option, e.g. `${1|!!!:static (always shown),???:collapsible closed,???+:collapsible open|}` (would require choosing a separator that does not clash with values). - A richer object form in the snippet JSON for choices, mapping each value to a description. ## Why Choice placeholders are often cryptic markers (here: Material/MkDocs admonition markers `!!!` / `???` / `???+`). Descriptions would let snippet authors document each option in place, exactly like the description already shown for the snippet as a whole.
input.title: Feature request: allow per-option descriptions in snippet choice placeholders (`${1|...|}`)

Type: Bug Setting "scm.diffDecorations": "none" in settings.json does not remove inline diff decorations from the editor. The old (strikethrough) and new (arrow + text) code changes are still visible inline inside the editor, making the code unreadable. Steps to Reproduce: 1. Have a file tracked by Git with uncommitted changes 2. Open settings.json and set "scm.diffDecorations": "none" 3. Reload VS Code 4. Open any modified file Expected Behavior: No diff decorations should appear in the editor. Actual Behavior: Inline diff decorations still show ΓÇö deleted lines appear as strikethrough text and new lines appear with an arrow (ΓåÆ) prefix, overlapping the actual code. Environment: - OS: Windows - VS Code Version: (add your version ΓÇö Help ΓåÆ About) - Extensions: GitLens Possible Cause: GitLens may be overriding the scm.diffDecorations setting and rendering its own inline change decorations independently. Workaround Attempted: - Set "scm.diffDecorations": "none" ΓåÆ no effect - Set "gitlens.changes.locations": [] ΓåÆ no effect VS Code version: Code 1.125.1 (fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353, 2026-06-19T08:39:34Z) OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 Modes:

Type: Bug 1. Launch VS Code with a custom extensions directory using the CLI argument, for example: code --extensions-dir "path/to/your/custom/directory" 2. Install several extensions and use VS Code normally. 3. Wait for a new VS Code update to be released and let VS Code perform the update. 4. After the update, launch VS Code again. (Note: The extensions are usually still present during the first 1 or 2 launches immediately after the update). 5. Continue using VS Code. After a few more launches (usually randomly within a day or two), launch VS Code again. 6. Result: All installed extensions suddenly disappear, and the custom extensions directory is found to be completely wiped/cleared. input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

**Best fit: Hooks + MCP Servers**

Org-managed policy distribution and audit are missing for Copilot agent workflows.

Local setup is not enough for teams that need governance. They need policy that can be pushed centrally, versioned, and audited across repos.

The first version should support:
- org-level policy packs
- policy version visibility
- override visibility
- audit of agent changes and policy decisions

This is a governance gap, not just a UI gap.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766
input.title: Org-managed policy distribution and audit

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

**Best fit: Instructions + Agents**

VS Code needs a real place for reusable agent instructions and rule packs.

Today, this is scattered across files and manual setup, which makes it hard to know what rules are active or keep them consistent across repos.

A native surface should let users:
- manage repo-level instructions
- reuse shared presets
- see what rules are active
- override defaults when needed

If this stays file-only, it will remain hard to discover and hard to govern.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766
input.title: Native customization surface for rule packs and instructions

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

**Best fit: Agents**

Agents need a visible guardrail status while they run.

Right now, itΓÇÖs too easy for an agent to look ΓÇ£doneΓÇ¥ when checks havenΓÇÖt run, have failed, or have been bypassed. That makes the workflow feel untrustworthy.

At minimum, the Agents area should show:
- whether required checks ran
- whether they passed or failed
- what was auto-fixed
- what is advisory vs blocking
- why the agent cannot finish yet

Without this, guardrails stay hidden and users have to guess what happened.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766
input.title: Guardrail status in the Agent window

<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- ≡ƒò« Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- ≡ƒöÄ Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- ≡ƒº¬ Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- ≡ƒÆí Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- ≡ƒöº Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

<!-- ≡ƒ¬ô If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- ≡ƒôú Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->

i previously opened #292016 regarding the extremely frustrating behavior where user-level `editor.defaultFormatter` config overrides workspace config. it has continued to annoy me since it was closed. to re-iterate what i said there, i cannot imagine a single situation where a user would *ever* want their user-level formatting config to override the project's. so this behavior seems completely nonsensical to me.

regardless, i thought i could workaround the problem by simply always specifying the languages in my project config, as was pointed out in https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/292016#issuecomment-3834718551, but even this does not seem to work correctly. 

specifically, it seems that when the workspace specifies more languages in its overrides, the user-level config incorrectly takes precedence.

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1
- OS Version: linux

Steps to Reproduce:

in user config:

```json
{
  "[javascript]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode"
   }
}
```

in project config:

```json
{
  "[javascript][typescript]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "dprint.dprint"
  }
}
```

when saving a `.js`  file in my project, it incorrectly uses prettier instead of dprint, even though there is a project-level language override for dprint
input.title: user-level settings for language overrides incorrectly override workspace language overrides when the workspace language override applies to more languages

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

Would really like to have it so I can collapse any or all of the prompts with all of the output from the chat. It's easy to scroll back through and find a prompt, something you had to move on from, something else now you need to go back to it. You can read the prompt that you asked and then open up the results from the chat. Since there's no way to search anything, you spend an inordinate amount of time scrolling through pages and pages and pages trying to find something, where this shouldn't be this difficult To find a previous prompt
input.title: Could we add collapsable sections to Chat prompts and a search option

input.body:
Follow-up to #322564.

In an untrusted workspace, Chat presents the user as **signed out even when signed in**, because auth/entitlement comes from the Copilot extension, which is disabled in untrusted workspaces. Symptoms:

1. "Sign In" button shows in title bar / accounts menu though signed in.
2. After clicking **Trust WorkspaceΓÇª**, the "Sign in to use GitHub Copilot" dialog flashes for ~5ΓÇô6s, then disappears once the extension activates and entitlement resolves.
3. ~5ΓÇô6s delay before models load / chat is usable after Trust.

**Root cause:** `chatEntitlementService.resolve()` reads `IDefaultAccountService.getDefaultAccount()`, whose provider is set by the Copilot extension. Untrusted ΓçÆ extension off ΓçÆ no account ΓçÆ entitlement `Unknown` (signed-out). After Trust, `chatSetupRunner.doRun()` (`:114`) decides the sign-in dialog from this still-unresolved entitlement, so it shows the modal until the extension activates.

**Regression?**
- The architecture (auth/entitlement/models gated on the trust-gated extension) is pre-existing. Increased *exposure* is a regression from no longer showing the startup workspace-trust dialog (see #322564).
- The specific UI bugs (Sign In affordances ignoring untrusted; dialog decided from unresolved entitlement) are pre-existing, **not** introduced by the model-picker PR.
- The model-load delay is inherent activation/network latency, not a regression.

**Deterministic "pending vs signed-out" signal exists:** `defaultAccountStatus` = `Uninitialized` (`defaultAccount.ts:61,66`), `DefaultAccountProvider.initialized`/`initPromise` (`:292,317-320`), and `ChatEntitlement.Unresolved` vs `Unknown` (`chatEntitlementService.ts:81-82,453-457`).

**Proposed follow-up:**
1. In `doRun()`, after Trust from untrusted, await account/entitlement resolution before the `:114` dialog decision so already-signed-in users skip the dialog.
2. Make the Sign In affordances untrusted-aware at the source. The model-picker PR adds `Setup.untrusted.negate()` guards in `chatSetupContributions.ts` as a stopgap; the source fix makes them redundant.
input.title: Chat appears signed-out in untrusted workspaces (Sign In button + post-Trust sign-in dialog)

input.body:
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Not tested ΓÇö issue is in
GitHub Copilot Chat extension (the `memory` tool is provided by that extension),
so disabling it disables the feature under test.

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1
- OS Version: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 (Plow), kernel 5.14.0-570.84.1.el9_6.x86_64

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open agent mode in GitHub Copilot Chat with a memory file > 200 lines (any
   markdown file under `/memories/` accessible via the `memory` tool).
2. Invoke the `memory` tool with `command: "str_replace"` and a `newString`
   parameter containing ~5300 characters / ~75 lines of markdown ΓÇö content
   should include section headers (`###`), nested bullet lists, inline code
   spans (backticks), and a markdown table (` | ` separators).
3. The tool returns success with no error message.
4. Invoke `memory` with `command: "view"` on the same file and inspect the
   replacement location.

**Expected:** the full `newString` content is written verbatim at the location
of `oldString`.

**Actual:** the literal 9-character string `"undefined"` (without quotes) is
written at the replacement location. The `oldString` is correctly removed, and
content before and after the replacement point survives intact ΓÇö only the
`newString` payload is dropped and replaced with `undefined`. This is the
classic JavaScript `undefined.toString()` coercion fingerprint, suggesting
the `newString` parameter is dropped/nulled at the JSON-RPC serialization
boundary between the language model and the tool host.

**Workaround:** split `newString` into sequential Γëñ 2000-character chunks
and call `memory str_replace` / `memory insert` once per chunk. Each
sub-2000-char call succeeds. Empirically, the threshold appears to be in
the 4096ΓÇô5300 char range ΓÇö values below ~4000 always succeed; values
above ~5000 always fail with the `undefined` coercion.

**Reproducibility:** confirmed across 3 distinct incidents in a single
agent-mode session on 2026-06-23, all with `newString` between 4500 and
5800 characters of markdown.

**Related observation:** in prior sessions, large `memory insert` calls
with multi-line backtick-fenced content produced a different but related
failure mode ΓÇö mid-file content duplication (a header block was injected
into an unrelated section of the same file). Both failures may share the
same serialization-boundary root cause; size threshold and content
structure both appear to influence which mode triggers.

**Impact:** silent data loss. The tool returns success, the agent
assumes the write succeeded, and the corruption is only detected when
the agent (or user) next reads the file. In one incident the corrupted
content was a critical workflow rule; recovery required rebuilding
from a separate source-of-truth file.

**Suggested fix areas to investigate:**
- JSON-RPC max-payload limit on tool parameters between model and host
- Possible truncation in the request serializer that produces a valid
  but empty/undefined parameter rather than rejecting the call
- The tool host's parameter validator should reject (not coerce)
  `undefined` for a required string parameter
input.title: Memory str_replace tool silently drops large newString values (coerces to string "undefined")

Type: Performance Issue Window: Agents Dear Help Desk, I'm reporting a systematic reliability problem with the Copilot CLI agent in my VS Code workflow. The Pattern: Over multiple sessions, the agent has been asked to follow a mandatory startup gate (documented in project archives) before performing any work. This gate requires reading critical system documentation to prevent repeated failures. The agent consistently skips this gate, attempts work from incomplete knowledge, fails multiple times, and only succeeds after repeated correction and escalation. Today's Example: Task: Fix website cache configuration Correct method: Read project-specific documentation first, then execute deployment workflow What the agent did: Made assumptions about deployment method, attempted multiple incorrect approaches over several iterations, wasted significant time and tokens Result: Only succeeded after being forced to read the actual documentation The Core Problem: The agent admits it will repeatedly make this mistake because: No enforcement mechanism prevents skipping the startup gate The agent is biased toward speed over accuracy In every future session, the same pattern will repeat Success requires multiple failed attempts and user escalation before the agent reads required documentation When asked for a solution, the agent acknowledged: No enforcement structure will fix this. It will rationalize around any rule. Logical Conclusion: Given that the failures are documented, repeated identically across sessions, and require user escalation to correct, it is a logical conclusion that the system is designed to not work properly and to waste time and money repeatedly. Recommendation: Do not use this tool for production work at all. Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open a workspace with a Git remote.
2. Open the Source Control view and select the "Files" tab (where modified files are listed).
3. Observe modified files (they show the modified symbol) ΓÇö previously there was a simple button in this view to pull down changes from the remote.
4. The button to pull/fetch remote changes from the Files tab is no longer present.

Observed Behavior:

- Modified files show the modified symbol, but there is no simple button in the Files tab to pull or fetch remote changes as there used to be.

Expected Behavior:

- A clear "Pull"/"Fetch" action/button should be available in the Files tab (or next to modified files) to pull down changes from the remote repository, as previously provided.

Additional context:

- This appears to be a regression in the Files tab UI ΓÇö the user previously had a single-button flow to pull changes directly from the Files tab and that control is missing now. No other details were provided in the original report.

input.body:
**VS Code version:** Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)
Commit: fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353
Date: 2026-06-19T08:39:34Z
Electron: 42.2.0
ElectronBuildId: 14159160
Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
Node.js: 24.15.0
V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26100
**Extension:** built-in `copilot` extension (`resources/app/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js`)

### Summary

During workspace startup, the extension-host main thread is frozen for **~14 seconds inside a single synchronous `require()`** triggered by the built-in Copilot extension's first HTTPS request. Because the entire extension host shares one JS thread, this blocks **all** other extensions' timers, microtasks, and I/O callbacks for the duration. In my case it delayed the Pylance language client from sending its `initialize` request to the (already-spawned) language server by ~14ΓÇô60s, making the language server appear to "hang" at startup.

### Root cause

When Copilot opens its **first** HTTPS connection, the TLS handshake completes and `undici` **lazily `require()`s its HTTP/1 parser (`llhttp`)** from inside the connection callback. That `require` is a **synchronous CommonJS module load** (`readFileSync` ΓåÆ `readFileUtf8`). On Windows the read appears to be heavily amplified by Defender real-time scanning and disk contention from other extensions spawning child processes at startup, stretching this one synchronous load to ~14s.

A synchronous `require` cannot yield, so for those ~14s the event loop runs **zero** timers/microtasks/I-O callbacks ΓÇö every other extension and any language client running in the host is starved.

### Evidence (CPU profile)

The startup window (0ΓÇô35s) is **~95% CPU-saturated** (33.4s busy / 35s). Within it there is a **single contiguous 13.9s run (+7.8s ΓåÆ +21.7s)** where `readFileSync`/`readFileUtf8` is the leaf on the stack, and **100% of the samples in that run (8315/8315) root to the `copilot` extension**. Representative stack (leaf ΓåÆ root):

```
readFileUtf8                         [native]
readFileSync                         [node:fs]
t.readFileSync                       [node:electron/js2c/node_init]
defaultLoadImpl / loadSource         [node:internal/modules/cjs/loader]
Module._extensions..js / Module.load [node:internal/modules/cjs/loader]
Module._load / wrapModuleLoad        [node:internal/modules/cjs/loader]
Module.require                       [node:internal/modules/cjs/loader]
require                              [resources/app/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js]
lazyllhttp                           [node_modules/undici/lib/dispatcher/client-h1.js]
connectH1                            [node_modules/undici/lib/dispatcher/client-h1.js]
onConnectSecure                      [node:internal/tls/wrap]
TLSSocket._finishInit                [node:internal/tls/wrap]
ssl.onhandshakedone                  [node:internal/tls/wrap]
(root)
```

During this whole window the affected language client received only ~0.6s of CPU ΓÇö i.e. it was effectively frozen, not doing async work of its own.

### Impact

- Any extension or language client sharing the extension host stalls for the duration of the synchronous load.
- Symptom observed: a language server that has already spawned sits idle for up to ~60s before receiving `initialize`, looking like a hang.
- Worse on Windows due to Defender scanning + concurrent child-process disk contention at startup.

### Suggested mitigations

1. **Pre-warm undici's `llhttp` asynchronously** (or eagerly `import()` it) before/at activation, off the hot connection path, so the first HTTPS request doesn't trigger a synchronous module load on the shared thread.
2. Avoid issuing the first network request synchronously during the busiest part of startup, or defer it until after the host has settled.
3. (Upstream) consider an async `llhttp` load path in `undici` so `connectH1` doesn't `require()` synchronously inside the TLS callback.

### Workaround for users

Adding a Microsoft Defender exclusion for the VS Code install directory noticeably reduces the synchronous read time.
input.title: Extension host frozen ~14s by synchronous require() of undici llhttp on Copilot's first HTTPS request (starves other extensions / language clients at startup)

input.body:
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0 (user setup)
- Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a chat/agent session that has an auto-generated title.
2. Open the sessions list (`Chat: Show Chats...`) and rename the session to a custom name (e.g. "Access Management and PR reviews").
3. The new name appears to save.
4. Close and reopen the session (or reload the window).

## Expected

The custom title persists.

## Actual

The title reverts to the previous auto-generated name as soon as the session is reopened. The manual rename does not persist.

## Notes

- Reproduces both when renaming from the open tab and from the sessions list.
- `chat.sessionSync.enabled` is enabled (default); unsure if sync is overwriting the local rename.

## Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Not tested
input.title: Renamed chat session title reverts to auto-generated name on reopen (1.126.0)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a44b585c-2a76-4679-a851-9d1693e00632

* Open VS Code fresh
* Sign in via the Modal
* see all models, great.
* Now open a folder (note the Untrusted Workspace)
* Open models
* ≡ƒÉ¢ only see `Auto`

Now imagine I went straight to a workspace... you could use VS Code for the very first time and think that you don't have access to models.

We need to be clear about this so that the user doesn't think that they don't have access to models.

This started because we no longer show the workspace trust dialog at start.
input.title: It needs to be clear that the reason I only see Auto is because I'm in an untrusted repository

input.body:
## Problem

EDU/Student users currently appear to be routed through the same quota messaging as the `individual` plan. In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/chatErrorMessages.ts`, `ChatEntitlement.EDU` maps to `copilotPlan: 'individual'`, and the quota messaging for `individual` tells users to upgrade to **Copilot Pro+**.

For EDU/Student users, we should nudge them to **Copilot Pro** instead.

## Expected

When EDU/Student users hit Copilot limits, the upgrade messaging should direct them to **Copilot Pro**, not **Copilot Pro+**.

## Notes

Relevant code paths:
- `getCopilotPlanFromEntitlement(ChatEntitlement.EDU)` currently returns `'individual'`
- `getQuotaMessageForPlan('individual', ...)` uses messaging that says to upgrade to Copilot Pro+
input.title: Nudge EDU/Student users to Copilot Pro instead of Pro+ when limits are reached

input.body:
I use `Remote-SSH: Connect to Host ...` to a remote Windows WSL2 session (via OpenVPN).

- If I revert to versions <= 1.122.1, the remote connection properly works.
- On versions 1.123 to 1.125.1 it fails to properly connect, with 1.125.1 stating "Failed to set up dynamic port forwarding connection"
- On 1.125.1 the remote files are shown in Explorer, but ultimately cannot connect.

---

I diffed the startup logs. The recent versions immediately show this error after spawning SSH (v1.125.1):

```
[16:04:24.854] stderr> local-server-1> listen EINVAL: invalid argument /var/folders/z_/tydm3n9d46d17g1qnd2ddnfm0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-1e83c16a43d3969848e1bccc6f0f598ae130826b.sock
[16:04:25.300] Server delay-shutdown request failed: connect EINVAL /var/folders/z_/tydm3n9d46d17g1qnd2ddnfm0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-1e83c16a43d3969848e1bccc6f0f598ae130826b.sock - Local (undefined:undefined)
```

At the very end, here is the successful log (v1.122.1):
```
[17:54:02.457] Resolved "ssh-remote+remote-wsl" to "port 63670"
[17:54:02.461] Initizing new exec server for ssh-remote+remote-wsl
[17:54:02.462] Resolving exec server at port 63670
[17:54:02.462] [Forwarding server port 63670] Got connection 0
[17:54:02.463] [Forwarding server port 63670] Got connection 1
[17:54:02.899] Exec server for ssh-remote+remote-wsl created and cached
[17:54:02.900] Extensions to install: 
[17:54:02.900] Remote extension host environment: {"DISPLAY":":0"}
[17:54:02.908] ------
```

versus the unsuccessful log from recent versions (v1.125.1):
```
[16:04:46.442] Resolved "ssh-remote+remote-wsl" to "port 59585"
[16:04:46.442] Resolving exec server at port 59585
[16:04:46.442] [Forwarding server port 59585] Got connection 0
[16:04:46.443] [Forwarding server port 59585] Got connection 1
[16:04:46.443] Failed to set up socket for dynamic port forward to remote port 46195: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:59573. TCP port forwarding may be disabled, or the remote server may have crashed. See the VS Code Server log above for details.
[16:04:46.444] Failed to set up socket for dynamic port forward to remote port 46195: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:59573. TCP port forwarding may be disabled, or the remote server may have crashed. See the VS Code Server log above for details.
[16:04:48.004] Server delay-shutdown request failed: connect EINVAL /var/folders/z_/tydm3n9d46d17g1qnd2ddnfm0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-3a57135221a9b2c0073de3f6151999bcb4a72b28.sock - Local (undefined:undefined)
[16:04:48.566] > local-server-5> Timed out
[16:04:48.577] Local server exit: 0
```

input.body:
## Title
Excessive Debug Logging in Extension Host - InlineEditRequestLogContext

## Issue Type
Bug / Performance Issue

## Description
The GitHub Copilot extension is outputting excessive debug logging to the VSCode developer console, which is polluting the console output and potentially impacting performance.

## Console Output
```
console.ts:139 [Extension Host] [InlineEditRequestLogContext] outcome transition from 'skipped' to 'skipped' (request #5)
log @ console.ts:139
$logExtensionHostMessage @ mainThreadConsole.ts:45
_doInvokeHandler @ rpcProtocol.ts:458
_invokeHandler @ rpcProtocol.ts:443
_receiveRequest @ rpcProtocol.ts:373
_receiveOneMessage @ rpcProtocol.ts:299
∩╝êσî┐σÉì∩╝ë @ rpcProtocol.ts:161
_deliver @ event.ts:1364
fire @ event.ts:1395
fire @ ipc.net.ts:658
∩╝êσî┐σÉì∩╝ë @ localProcessExtensionHost.ts:421

console.ts:139 [Extension Host] [InlineEditRequestLogContext] outcome transition from 'skipped' to 'skipped' (request #7)
log @ console.ts:139
$logExtensionHostMessage @ mainThreadConsole.ts:45
_doInvokeHandler @ rpcProtocol.ts:458
_invokeHandler @ rpcProtocol.ts:443
_receiveRequest @ rpcProtocol.ts:373
_receiveOneMessage @ rpcProtocol.ts:299
∩╝êσî┐σÉì∩╝ë @ rpcProtocol.ts:161
_deliver @ event.ts:1364
fire @ event.ts:1395
fire @ ipc.net.ts:658
∩╝êσî┐σÉì∩╝ë @ localProcessExtensionHost.ts:421
```

## Impact

1. **Console Pollution**: The debug logs are cluttering the developer console, making it difficult to identify real issues
2. **Performance Concern**: Frequent logging may impact extension performance
3. **User Experience**: Non-technical users may be confused or concerned by these messages
4. **Debug Interference**: These logs interfere with legitimate debugging activities

## Expected Behavior

Debug logs should either:
- Be removed entirely from production builds
- Be enabled only in debug/verbose mode
- Use VSCode's logging API instead of `console.log`
- Have a higher threshold before outputting

## Actual Behavior

Debug logs are being output to the console on every inline edit request, regardless of the operation outcome.

## Environment

- **VSCode Version**: last_version
- **GitHub Copilot Extension Version**: last_version
- **OS**: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0)
- **Reproducibility**: Consistent - occurs frequently during normal usage

## Suggested Fix

1. Remove debug `console.log` statements from production code
2. Implement a logging level system that can be controlled via settings
3. Use VSCode's built-in logging API with appropriate log levels
4. Consider using telemetry for internal tracking instead of console output

## Additional Context

This appears to be a leftover from development/debugging that was not cleaned up before release. The `[InlineEditRequestLogContext]` tag suggests this is related to the inline editing feature's request handling logic.

## Labels

- bug
- logging
- performance
- quality

---
input.title: Excessive Debug Logging in Extension Host - InlineEditRequestLogContext

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Modify chatLanguageModels.json like this:

`{
		"name": "Custom Model",
		"vendor": "customendpoint",
		"apiType": "chat-completions",
		"models": [
			{

2. When I try to use it, it does not take the token even when its added in .bashrc. When I give directly as "Bearer abc123" , then it works fine. 

I want to keep BYOK-as-a-code and configurable via script, but this api key limitation forces to manually go and update api key which then saves it as secret.

I understand keeping it as secret is good w.r.t security aspect, but does not serve our particular usecase above.

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

We want to use custom agents for some requirements but are unable to use inbuilt browser tools because workbench.browser.enableChatTools shows as disabled. This setting is managed by organization and due to "Preview Feature" policy disabled on Github.com by organization, we are unable to use this tool.

Due to this, we have no choice but to use playwright mcp separately which bloats the context window. This feature was launched in March. When is the plan to move it from an "Experimental" to "Stable" featureset. I would assume once its no longer under Experimental feature, it should be controllable on User Level instead of org level. 

Its a useful feature and I believe it makes no sense to still keep it an experimental state or leave it to be managed by org policy.
input.title: Please remove workbench.browser.enableChatTools from experimental state

input.body:
We disabled the `Github Authentication Provider` Extension (`--disable-extension vscode.github-authentication`), as we are using BYOK with a llama.cpp Model (`customendpoint`) and do not want any data accidentally sent to GitHub.

When using the `semantic_search` tool, the following is shown in the Output tab of GitHub Copilot Chat:
```
Error: Timed out waiting for authentication provider 'github' to register.
    at iit.tryActivateProvider (vscode-file://vscode-app/usr/share/code/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js:1106:80425)
    at async iit.getSessions (vscode-file://vscode-app/usr/share/code/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js:1106:76403)
    at async iit.getAccounts (vscode-file://vscode-app/usr/share/code/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js:1106:76127)
    at async CVt.$getAccounts (vscode-file://vscode-app/usr/share/code/resources/app/out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js:1109:15283)
2026-06-23 10:14:37.550 [warning] [LanguageModelAccess] LanguageModel/Embeddings are not available without a Copilot token source
```
And the chat abruptly stops with:
```
Timed out waiting for authentication provider 'github' to register.
```

Instead of just stopping, it should send the regular error message.

- VS Code Version: 1.125.0
- OS Version: Fedora 44
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): agent
- Selected model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Thinking-UDQ6XXL

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Start VSCode using `code --disable-extension vscode.github-authentication`, configure a BYOK model
2. Activate the "search -> codebase" tool and use the chat in agent mode with"Search for the chat service, use semantic_search"
input.title: GitHub Copilot Chat: semantic_search tool stops chat if Github Authentication Provider is disabled

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

I am a user who has a repo with a devcontainer. I have designed the container to require no dependencies on startup (can start in a fresh directory without concern). I frequently want to work on multiple features and utilize `Clone Repository into Container Volume` for this. However, this requires me to type in the git repo url every single time I want to do this.

I am requesting a faster way to handle the workflow of creating another ephemeral devcontainer of a previously used repository. 

I have two proposed paths to achieve a more efficient workflow for this:
* Having a dropdown of recently used repositories (with potentially a way to star ones to always show up) that shows under the git url field in the existing `Clone Repository into Container Volume` popup. These values would need to be persisted at the level of the user running VSCode
* The ability to "open a fork of my current devcontainer": If I am already in a devcontainer, support detecting the repo that is in use for it and support another copy of the repo directly into another ephemeral devcontainer
input.title: Clone Repository in Container Volume list previously cloned repositories

Need produce final answer only. "}},"provider_specific_fields":{"token_ids":null,"routed_experts":null,"stop_reason":163586}}],"usage":{"completion_tokens":23,"prompt_tokens":9,"total_tokens":32}}

Moreover, I used two other applications, Jan.ai and Hermes to see if I face the same issue, but it works perfectly without any errors. I was able to see both the thinking trace and the actual output message content.

It would be great to see if there are any fixes or workarounds since the provider I am using is my university and they host latest models that we would want to use.

input.body: **VS Code version:** 1.125.1 **Steps to reproduce:** 1. Search `@agentPlugins` in the Extensions activity bar 2. Install the **Datadog** plugin from the awesome-copilot marketplace 3. Open chat and type `/` to see available skills **Expected:** Skills appear as `/datadog:ddsetup`, `/datadog:ddconfig`, `/datadog:ddtoolsets` (using the plugin `name` + skill `name` frontmatter fields) **Actual:** Skills appear as `/sha_b003fcad48c3a935ffe04b6218f5cf58fe2b6760...` ΓÇö the commit SHA from the marketplace entry **Root cause:** The awesome-copilot `marketplace.json` pins the Datadog plugin to a specific commit SHA. When VS Code installs from a SHA-pinned entry, it uses the commit SHA as the skill name prefix instead of the skill's `name` frontmatter field. The plugin itself is correctly structured ΓÇö skill `name` fields match their directory names, and `plugin.json` is well-formed: - `skills/ddsetup/SKILL.md` has `name: ddsetup` - `skills/ddconfig/SKILL.md` has `name: ddconfig` - `skills/ddtoolsets/SKILL.md` has `name: ddtoolsets` - `plugin.json` has `name: datadog` Source: [`datadog-labs/vscode-plugin`](https://github.com/datadog-labs/vscode-plugin) at `b003fcad48c3a935ffe04b6218f5cf58fe2b6760` Reported by @jfree.
input.title: Agent plugin skills show commit SHA as slash command name when installed from SHA-pinned marketplace entry

input.body:
When working in a project located on a **slow network directory**, VS Code appears to trigger a rapid increase in the number of **threads and processes** on the system. This happens especially when trying to save a file while directory scanning/background loading is still ongoing. In that situation, VS Code seems to generate even more activity against the slow network location, and resource usage starts climbing aggressively. Over time, the machine becomes heavily loaded and responsiveness degrades significantly. It looks like VS Code may be issuing too many requests against the slow network location, eventually causing resource usage to "blow up" and effectively burn CPU/system resources. I cannot rule out that part of this is related to the network client itself, but the steadily increasing number of requests against the slow machine seems likely to be triggered by the editor. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Open a folder/workspace located on a slow network share / network-mounted directory. 2. Let VS Code start scanning/loading the directory. 3. While that scanning/loading is still in progress, try to save a file. 4. Observe system resource usage in Windows Task Manager over time. 5. Notice that the number of processes/threads/handles keeps increasing instead of staying stable. ### Actual result - Process/thread/handle counts keep growing while VS Code is open on the slow network directory. - The problem is more likely / more severe when saving a file during ongoing directory scanning. - System CPU usage and overall load increase noticeably. - The machine becomes unstable/sluggish. - Even after closing VS Code, the system may remain elevated for a while before dropping again. ### Expected result - VS Code should handle slow network directories gracefully. - Saving a file during background scanning should not trigger runaway activity. - Background activity should be throttled or bounded. - Process/thread/request counts should not grow unbounded when the workspace is on a slow network location. ### Notes - The attached screenshots show Task Manager values rising over time while VS Code is open. - The last screenshot is after closing VS Code. - This may involve file watching, directory scanning, search, extension host activity, or repeated retries against the network path.

### Version Version: 1.125.1 (user setup) Commit: fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353 Date: 2026-06-19T08:39:34Z Electron: 42.2.0 ElectronBuildId: 14159160 Chromium: 148.0.7778.97 Node.js: 24.15.0 V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200
input.title: Runaway thread/process growth when opening a workspace on a slow network share

input.body:
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: built-in VS Code `copilot` extension from `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\fcf604774b\resources\app\extensions\copilot`; `code --list-extensions --show-versions` does not list `GitHub.copilot-chat`
- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (`fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353`, x64)
- OS Version: Windows 11 Pro 25H2, OS build 26200.8246, Windows Feature Experience Pack 1000.26100.297.0
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Chat panel, Ask mode / normal chat response rendering
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Custom API via UnifyChatProvider, GPT-5.5
- Logs: I have an exported `chat.json`; relevant excerpt is included below.

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open VS Code with a workspace nested inside a named parent folder, for example:

   ```text
   C:\Users\<user>\Desktop\repro-parent\repro-workspace
   ```

2. Open Copilot Chat.

3. Ask Copilot Chat to repeat a command containing `./...` inside inline code:

   ```text
   Please repeat this exact command in inline code: `go test ./...`
   ```

4. If it does not reproduce on the first try, add workspace/file context and ask again:

   ```text
   The correct command is `go test ./...`. Repeat only the command in inline code.
   ```

Expected:

The chat response should visibly render exactly:

```text
go test ./...
```

Actual:

The stored model/tool-round response is correct, but the rendered chat message can replace `./..` with an inline file/folder reference to the parent directory and leave the final `.` as text.

In my case the workspace was under:

```text
C:\Users\gyli\Desktop\Misc-local\pylink\jlink-cli
```

The parent folder was `pylink`, so the UI rendered the command as:

```text
go test pylink.
```

Evidence from the exported chat JSON:

The stored final response for request 8 is correct:

```json
"response": "┬╡┬í├║╧ä├¡┬½╧â├Ñ├á╧â┬½Γòú┬╡├┐┬╗Γê⌐Γò¥├£\n\n`go test ./...`"
```

But the rendered response parts were split into text + inlineReference + text:

```json
[
  { "value": "┬╡┬í├║╧ä├¡┬½╧â├Ñ├á╧â┬½Γòú┬╡├┐┬╗Γê⌐Γò¥├£\n\n`go test " },
  {
    "kind": "inlineReference",
    "inlineReference": {
      "fsPath": "c:\\Users\\gyli\\Desktop\\Misc-local\\pylink",
      "path": "/C:/Users/gyli/Desktop/Misc-local/pylink",
      "scheme": "file"
    }
  },
  { "value": ".`" }
]
```

This appears to turn `./...` into:

```text
./..  -> inlineReference to parent folder "pylink"
.     -> remaining literal dot
```

Impact:

- The visible chat output changes the literal content of a command inside inline code.
- It makes users think the model generated the wrong command even when the stored response is correct.
- `go test ./...` is a common Go command, so this is easy to hit in development chats.
- The same issue may affect other command tokens containing `./...` or similar relative path syntax.

Possible implementation clue:

This looks related to chat inline reference annotation/linkification rather than model output. It may be near chat inline reference handling in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/widget/annotations.ts` or `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatContentParts/chatMarkdownContentPart.ts`. There appears to already be coverage for inline references inside backtick code spans, but `./...` may be a missing edge case where `./..` is recognized as a path reference before/while code-span handling runs.
input.title: Copilot Chat renders `./...` inside inline code as a parent-folder inline reference

input.body:
Currently, VS Code has two separate systems for discovering Copilot agent files:

Workbench-side (.agent.md files): Already configurable via the chat.agent.fileLocations setting.
Agent host-side (Copilot SDK session discovery in sessionCustomizationDiscovery.ts): Paths are hardcoded and cannot be customized.
The agent host side scans a fixed set of directories for agents, skills, and instruction files:

Workspace:

.github/agents, .agents/agents, .claude/agents (agents)
.github/skills, .agents/skills, .claude/skills (skills)
.github/instructions (instructions)
User home:

.copilot/agents (agents)
.agents/skills (skills)
.copilot/instructions (instructions)
Instruction files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, etc.) are also discovered from hardcoded paths.

Problem
Teams and individuals who organize their repositories differently (e.g., monorepos, custom tooling conventions, or non-standard directory layouts) cannot point the agent host discovery to their preferred locations. There is no escape hatch ΓÇö the only option is to restructure the repository to match the hardcoded paths.

In contrast, the workbench-side already demonstrates that this is solvable: PromptsConfig.AGENTS_LOCATION_KEY allows users to specify custom paths for .agent.md files via settings. The agent host side lacks an equivalent mechanism.

Proposed Solution
Expose the agent host searchRoots (and optionally agentInstructions) as a VS Code setting, similar to how chat.agent.fileLocations works for the workbench side. For example:

// settings.json  
"chat.agent.hostDiscoveryPaths": {  
  "workspace": {  
    "agents": [".github/agents", "my-team/agents"],  
    "skills": [".github/skills"],  
    "instructions": [".github/instructions"]  
  },  
  "user": {  
    "agents": ["~/.copilot/agents", "~/.my-org/agents"]  
  }  
}
The setting should:

Accept relative paths (resolved from workspace root) and ~/-prefixed paths (resolved from user home).
Default to the current hardcoded values so existing behavior is preserved.
Be marked as restricted: true (workspace trust) consistent with the existing chat.agent.fileLocations setting.
Relevant Code
Hardcoded discovery: src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/sessionCustomizationDiscovery.ts (searchRoots, agentInstructions)
Configurable workbench-side equivalent: src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/promptSyntax/config/promptFileLocations.ts (DEFAULT_AGENT_SOURCE_FOLDERS)
Setting registration: src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/chat.shared.contribution.ts (AGENTS_LOCATION_KEY)
Use Cases
Monorepos where agents live in a subdirectory (e.g., packages/ai-tools/agents)
Organizations with a shared agents directory outside the standard .github/ convention
Users who want to keep agent files alongside their dotfiles in a non-default home directory path
input.title: [Feature Request] Allow configuring Copilot agent/skill/instruction discovery paths via settings

input.body:
Several Keyboard shortcuts (probably those including Shift key) for a custom editor does not work on VS Code for the Web.

An example is `markdown.togglePreview` ("Markdown: Toggle Preview"), whose default keyboard shortcut is Shift+Cmd+V for macOS and Shift+Ctrl+V for other platforms. This shortcut toggles the editor of a markdown file between the built-in editor and markdown preview on the VS Code desktop version. However, on VS Code for the Web this keyboard shortcut does not switch the editor from the markdown preview to the built-in editor. Selecting from Command Palette, it works as expected.

I checked this problem occurs on both Safari/macOS and Edge/Windows. The problem is reproduced on another custom editor. 

I also noticed that, pressing a Shift key on VS Code for the Web changes several icons on the editor toolbar, just like pressing an Option/Alt key. On VS Code desktop version, this kind of toolbar action toggling occurs only when an Option/Alt key is pressed. I guess a Shift key input may be intercepted by mistake on VS Code for the Web.

input.body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->
**Summary**

While using the Bring your own language model key feature in VS Code, the custom endpoint configuration does not currently provide a way to authenticate with a Bearer token in the same flexible way as the built-in Azure provider.

A key requirement is support for dynamic generation of a Bearer token and sending it with each request, similar to how the Azure provider handles authentication today.

**Requested enhancement**

Please add support for:
- Bearer token authentication for custom language model endpoints
- Dynamic token acquisition or generation
- Automatic inclusion of the token in the request headers, such as Authorization: Bearer <token>

This would enable authentication against OpenAI-compatible models exposed through an organization-managed AI gateway.

**Why this matters**

In enterprise environments, authentication is often handled centrally rather than through static API keys. A dynamic Bearer token flow is important because it allows:
- Integration with organizational identity and access systems
- Short-lived or refreshed tokens for improved security
- Support for gateways that broker access to multiple model providers
- A consistent experience with the Azure provider authentication model

**Expected behavior**

The custom endpoint configuration should support a mechanism where:
- A Bearer token can be generated dynamically at runtime, and
- That token is attached to outgoing requests automatically

This would make the feature compatible with enterprise AI gateways and other OpenAI-compatible services that rely on token-based authentication.

**Business value**
- Enables secure enterprise adoption
- Avoids dependence on static keys
- Aligns custom endpoint behavior with the Azure provider experience
- Expands support for organization-managed AI gateways and OpenAI-compatible endpoints
input.title: Bearer token authentication for custom language model endpoints

Co-Pilot View: Can we have a "Don't show again" for the "Credit limit reached"? I can only close it and it appears again. I want to close it permanently. I don't want to be notified about it. Add this to your list of features like everywhere else we have: "Don't show it again!"
input.title: Co-Pilot View: Can we have a "Don't show again" for the "Credit limit reached"?

<!-- ≡ƒ¬ô If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- ≡ƒôú Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)
- Commit: fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353
- Date: 2026-06-19T08:39:34Z
- Electron: 42.2.0
- ElectronBuildId: 14159160
- Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
- Node.js: 24.15.0
- V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open a project located in a directory (e.g., `e:\aaa\bbb`) that is initialized as a Git repository.
2. Rename the project's root folder using Windows File Explorer.
3. Open the newly renamed folder in VS Code and delete its `.git` folder to stop version control tracking.
4. The built-in Git extension crashes into an infinite, high-frequency loop running `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.
5. The Git output channel constantly spams thousands of lines saying `fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git`.
6. Immediately following, the extension model spams warnings: `[warning] [Model][getRepositoryExact] Failed to get repository realpath for: ... Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, realpath`.
7. Even if you reset workspace profiles, use `Developer: Reset State`, reload windows, close the folder completely, or temporarily rename the folder back to its original name, the internal path state remains desynchronized. If renamed back, it instead loops complaining that the "new" name cannot be found via `ENOENT`.

### Git Log Output Sample:
```text
2026-06-23 10:11:34.479 [info] > git rev-parse --show-toplevel [29ms]
2026-06-23 10:11:34.479 [info] fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git
2026-06-23 10:11:35.423 [warning] [Model][getRepositoryExact] Failed to get repository realpath for: "e:\aaa\bbb". Error:Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, realpath 'e:\aaa\bbb'
```
input.title: Git: getRepositoryExact loops infinitely with ENOENT error after workspace folder rename

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Have a markdown file with a link to a local pdf ie:
```
[link](./test.pdf#page=3)
```

2. Install a pdf preview extension, ie [tomoki1207](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=tomoki1207.pdf)
3. Preview the markdown
4. Click on the link

Result
pdf opens on the 1st page

Expected behavior:
*  extension should receive uri with hash information.
* pdf opens on the 3rd page

Not sure if this is a bug or a feature, 
More information: https://github.com/James-Yu/LaTeX-Workshop/issues/4409
input.title: vscode.Uri drops hash information

<!-- ≡ƒº¬ Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->

<!-- ≡ƒÆí Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->

<!-- ≡ƒöº Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->

**Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: YES
I ran SHIFT + CTRL + P and did "disable all installed extensions" and still saw copilot working with the terminal.

<!-- ≡ƒ¬ô If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->

<!-- ≡ƒôú Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0-insider
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Use Copilot Chat / agent mode to run one or more background terminal commands.
2. Let a background command complete while the terminal remains hidden.
3. Try to inspect, reveal, preserve, group, export, or otherwise audit that terminal execution after completion.
4. Compare the available post-completion affordances against what a user would need if the agent made a mistake, missed an error, or ran a destructive command that exited successfully.

---

## Actual Behavior

Completed hidden background terminals may be disposed/cleaned up automatically, leaving only [mirrored or snapshot-style output available in the chat UI](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323016).

This creates a user-agency and auditability problem. Chat agents do not always correctly understand or surface errors that happened in terminals. They may miss command failures, misinterpret output, overlook warnings, or continue as though an operation succeeded when the terminal output shows otherwise.

A terminal created by an agent is not merely a live process/PTY resource. It is also an execution record. It is evidence of what the agent actually did.

The current behavior appears to treat "hidden + completed" as a reasonable proxy for "safe to dispose." That is not safe enough.

A command can exit successfully and still be the exact execution the user later needs to inspect. For example:

- A destructive command may delete or move files and still exit with code 0.
- A script may mutate a repository, database, generated artifacts, permissions, ownership, branches, tags, or remotes.
- An install/build/migration command may complete while producing warnings or unintended changes.
- The agent may fail to notice that the command output contains an important error or warning.
- The user may only realize after the chat response that they need to inspect the terminal.

In these cases, the fact that the terminal was hidden at completion does not mean the user no longer needs it. The user should not lose post-hoc access to the execution artifact simply because they did not reveal the terminal before it finished.

---

## Expected Behavior

VS Code should separate terminal resource cleanup from terminal artifact retention.

It is reasonable to clean up live terminal/PTY/process resources so completed background terminals do not accumulate indefinitely.  Or present a background compromise vector. However, cleanup should **not** remove the user's ability to inspect, preserve, reveal, group, export, or **reason** about the execution afterward.

At minimum, completed agent terminal executions should remain available as first-class execution-history artifacts containing:

- Command executed
- Working directory
- Shell/session identity
- Exit code / signal / cancellation state
- Timestamps and duration
- Full output
- Whether the terminal was hidden, revealed, failed, cancelled, timed out, or disposed
- Relevant tool metadata
- Association with the chat turn/session that created it

There should also be user-facing affordances such as:

- Reveal related terminal/execution
- Show only terminals/executions related to the current chat
- Group related terminals by chat/session/turn
- Clear terminal history for the current chat, explicitly deactivating associated shells/resources
- Persist terminals for this chat/session
- Export/download/view chat history and terminal execution history together
- Pin important terminal executions
- Rerun or recreate a completed execution where practical

Terminal reuse should also be considered as a preferable alternative to immediate destruction in cases where reuse is safe and understandable. If the agent already sometimes reuses terminals, the lifecycle should be explicit and inspectable rather than hidden behind automatic disposal. Reuse has risks because shell state can persist, but those risks argue for clearer session management, not for silently removing evidence.

This is especially important for failed, cancelled, timed-out, nonzero-exit, suspicious, destructive, or high-impact commands. Those executions should be retained more aggressively, not cleaned up less visibly.

---

## The Provenance Graph Problem

Agent terminal executions are not isolated UI details. They are part of the causal history of the chat session. The product already has many of these edges at runtime:

```
chat turn
  ΓåÆ model response
  ΓåÆ tool call
  ΓåÆ terminal/session
  ΓåÆ command
  ΓåÆ cwd/env
  ΓåÆ stdout/stderr
  ΓåÆ exit status
  ΓåÆ files changed
  ΓåÆ user-visible result
  ΓåÆ follow-up agent claim
```

This is naturally a DAG. A user should be able to inspect the chain of events that led to a result:

- Which chat turn initiated the action
- Which model/tool decision created the terminal command
- Which terminal/session/shell executed it
- What command ran
- What working directory and relevant environment were used
- What output, warnings, and errors occurred
- What exit status resulted
- What files, repository state, generated artifacts, or external state may have changed
- What later agent claims depended on that execution

**Removing or collapsing the terminal artifact breaks this graph exactly where users most need traceability.**

For AI agent workflows, the safest default should be evidence preservation, not evidence disappearance. If storage or resource use is a concern, live shells can be deactivated, old records can be compacted, and retention policies can be explicit. But the execution record should not silently vanish merely because the terminal was hidden when the command completed.

A better design would provide a chat/session execution history view that lets users trace from an agent response back to the commands and terminal sessions that produced it. This could include:

- Grouping by chat turn
- Showing related terminals only
- Revealing terminal history
- Exporting chat plus terminal history together
- Preserving tombstones for disposed/deactivated shells

The goal should be simple: **when an agent does something, the user should be able to see what led to it. Agent actions should be more inspectable than ordinary user actions, not less.**

---

## On User Agency and Consent

The implicit design assumption underlying the current behavior appears to be that **users who did not reveal a terminal before it completed did not need it.**

This conflates *unawareness* with *consent*.

A user who did not know to reveal a terminal is not a user who chose not to preserve it. They are a user who did not yet know they would need it ΓÇö which is precisely the situation that post-hoc inspection exists to address.

Removing access to execution artifacts from users who did not know they needed them is not a neutral UX decision. It is a decision about whose judgment prevails when the agent's behavior and the user's interests diverge. The answer should not be "the agent's" ΓÇö and it should especially not be implemented as a silent default that requires users to have pre-revealed terminals before they knew they would need them.

An escape hatch that requires the user to act before they know they need it is not an escape hatch. It is an illusion of agency.

---

## Related Context

- [[#287177](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/287177)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/287177) ΓÇö appears to describe the legitimate resource accumulation problem
- [[#316278](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316278)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316278) ΓÇö implemented disposal of completed hidden background terminals
- [[#302109](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/302109)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/302109) ΓÇö user frustration when agent terminal output becomes unavailable or difficult to inspect
- [[#316492](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316492)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316492) ΓÇö appears to test/validate the new ephemeral behavior
- [[#321049](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321049)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321049) ΓÇö session-switch disposal of agent terminals (related, separate mechanism)
- [[#322121](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322121)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322121) ΓÇö partial fix: protects user-revealed terminals on session switch; hidden terminals remain subject to disposal

---

## Summary

The request here is not "never clean up background terminals."

The request is:

> **Clean up live resources without taking away the user's ability to inspect and preserve evidence of what the agent did.**

- Hidden is not abandoned.
- Completed is not worthless.
- A clean exit is not proof that the command was harmless.
- [A snapshot is not necessarily product-equivalent to an inspectable terminal execution artifact.](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323016)
- Removing the terminal breaks the provenance graph.

That last point is the core of this issue. You are not deleting a UI object. You are deleting a causal link in the user's ability to understand what their agent actually did.
input.title: Preserve user agency when cleaning up completed agent terminals

### Description Completions requests are not always sent to the debug adapter after every character typed. Reproduction steps: 1. Run the Mock Debug Adapter 2. Launch the "Debug readme.md" debug configuration 3. Start typing "item" in the debug console. I expect the debug adapter completions to show up after every character typed, but sometimes, they do not. This seems to be related to whether there is another source of completions or not. In the mock debug adapter, there's some suggestions for markdown formatting which show up, but in my custom debug adapter, history suggestions have caused this issue. Subsequently, with my custom debug adapter, if the "debug.console.historySuggestions" setting is turned off, then it works as expected. input.body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Untested Title: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes/No VS Code: 1.126.0-insider Commit: e6f4d6c6f2977850cdae6b9e53f706f3c5faa63b Date: 2026-06-21T19:43:34-07:00 OS: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 1. Open a VS Code workspace with one or more existing integrated terminals already running. 2. Start a Copilot/Agent session and ask it to perform work that requires terminal commands. 3. Observe whether the agent reuses an existing suitable terminal or creates a new terminal. 4. Repeat across multiple requests/sessions. Actual Behavior: When I ask agent sessions to do work, they mostly do not reuse existing terminals. Instead, they often create new terminals even when an appropriate existing terminal is already available. Expected Behavior: The agent should prefer reusing an existing suitable terminal when one is already available, especially when that terminal is already in the correct workspace, shell, environment, or remote session. Why this matters: This causes terminal clutter and can break workflow continuity. In some cases, the existing terminal has important context such as the correct working directory, SSH/session state, environment variables, Docker context, or previously prepared commands. Creating a new terminal instead of reusing the existing one makes agent-driven work less predictable. Frequency: This happens often / most of the time in my workflow. Additional Notes: This is especially noticeable during agentic coding sessions where I repeatedly ask the assistant to run commands, validate work, inspect output, or continue from prior terminal state. In the "terminal tabs" the terminal with a double icon was the only one that was reused.
input.title: Agent sessions favor creating new terminals instead of reusing existing terminals

input.body:
Type: Bug When I step in a multi-target debugging scenario, the source code and call stack should remain focused on the debug session/thread that I am stepping in. I am noticing that sometimes that is true, and sometimes the focus swaps to another debug session/thread that is stopped. For context, I am maintaining a custom debug adapter that performs remote debugging, so stepping can often take longer than 800ms. The behavior that I expect seems to conflict with the behavior that was introduced in these issues/commits and their followups: - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/commit/30d73ea6dfafaa65c6aef8194f988475f105336c - https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/125144#issuecomment-869641057 In order to keep both of these behaviors, here are some ideas: - Create a user setting to choose whether focus should be automatically passed to another thread or not when the current one starts running - Create a user setting to adjust the timeout for the automatic focus passing - Add a new DAP property for StoppedEvent or use the existing "preserveFocusHint" to change focus back to the stopped thread if the focus has been passed away Let me know if you want me to recreate this with the Mock Debug Adapter. input.body: I've been running local models in VS Code by configuring them in `chatLanguageModels.json` and they've worked fine for months. After the latest update, I only see one model in the list and next to it in grey text is "OpenAI Compatible (Deprecated)". First, what's going on? Are local models not allowed in VS Code anymore? Second, it doesn't work. I assume the warning is about a _future_ deprecation, not a current one because then why allow it to be selected as a model at all? I just get "Language model unavailable" when the process I've been doing for months when using these models has not changed, so I know it's not on my end. Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes - VS Code Version: - OS Version: Win 11 Pro 25H2

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open VS Code Insiders with Copilot/agents enabled.
2. Trigger model refresh (observed automatically when agents start).
3. Observe models refreshed log followed by a rate-limit error and the model picker disappearing.

input.body:
Type: Bug Quando uma a├º├úo do agente/Copilot dispara um comando Git que pede passphrase SSH, se eu abrir/focar o terminal somente depois que o prompt j├í apareceu, a entrada ├⌐ enviada automaticamente antes de eu terminar de digitar. Se eu deixo o terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt aparecer, a digita├º├úo funciona normalmente. Passos para reproduzir Iniciar uma a├º├úo via agente/Copilot que execute git commit e pe├ºa passphrase SSH. N├úo deixar o terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt. Quando aparecer Enter passphrase for "...id_ed25519", abrir/focar o terminal manualmente. Tentar digitar a passphrase. Comportamento atual A entrada ├⌐ auto-enviada cedo demais (n├úo consigo digitar a passphrase completa normalmente), causando falha de autentica├º├úo em algumas tentativas. Comportamento esperado O terminal deve aguardar a digita├º├úo completa da passphrase e s├│ enviar quando eu confirmar manualmente com Enter. Condi├º├úo comparativa importante Falha: terminal aberto/focado depois do prompt j├í vis├¡vel. Funciona: terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt aparecer. Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes

<!-- ≡ƒ¬ô If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- ≡ƒôú Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->
- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (Universal)
- OS Version: macos15 latest

Steps to Reproduce:


in `setModelConfiguration` :

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/cb128cbeffb5e3fda13c3fc6fa67e766eac408e6/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/languageModels.ts#L1211-L1217


## Bug Description

**Title:** `setModelConfiguration` writes per-model config to the wrong provider group when multiple groups share the same vendor

**Location:** `setModelConfiguration()` in languageModels.ts

**Root Cause:**

When a model has not yet been configured in any group (i.e., `group.settings` does not contain an entry for that model's `metadata.id`), the fallback logic picks the group to write to solely by vendor name:

```typescript
group = allGroups.find(g => g.vendor === metadata.vendor);
```

This picks the **first** group in the list that matches the vendor ΓÇö regardless of which group the model actually belongs to.

Additionally ΓÇö the first-step lookup has the same flaw (and runs before the fallback above):
```group = allGroups.find(g => g.vendor === metadata.vendor && g.settings?.[metadata.id] !== undefined);```
The settings key metadata.id is not unique across groups of the same vendor (two endpoints can serve the same model id). So when more than one same-vendor group already has an entry under that id, .find() returns the first match ΓÇö again regardless of which group the model actually belongs to ΓÇö and the fallback is never reached. Both steps resolve the group by vendor (+ model id) instead of consulting the authoritative modelΓåÆgroup mapping the service already maintains (_modelsGroups, where each entry carries modelIdentifiers + its owning group).

**Concrete Scenario:**

A user's `chatLanguageModels.json` has two provider groups for the same vendor (e.g., two separate API endpoints from the same third-party LM provider):

```json
{
  "name": "My Provider (endpoint-a)",
  "vendor": "myprovider",
  "settings": {
  	"deepseek-v4-pro": {
  		"reasoningEffort": "high"
  	}
  }
},
{
  "name": "My Provider (endpoint-b)",
  "vendor": "myprovider",
  "settings": {}
}
```

When the user sets a per-model configuration (e.g., context size) for a model belonging to the second group ("endpoint-b") via the model picker UI, `setModelConfiguration` is called. Since the second group has no prior settings for that model, the first-step lookup (`g.settings?.[metadata.id]`) fails. The fallback finds the first vendor-matching group ("endpoint-a") and writes the config there instead of to "endpoint-b".

**Impact:** Per-model settings land in the wrong group entry in `chatLanguageModels.json`, which can cause confusion, lost settings on reload, and cross-contamination between logically separate provider instances.
input.title: setModelConfiguration write per-model settings to the wrong provider group when multiple groups share the same vendor

input.body:
Refs: #321647

- [ ] macOS @TylerLeonhardt 
- [ ] Linux @rzhao271 

Complexity: 2

Author: @dileepyavan

---

**Context:** When an agent requests to run a terminal command outside the sandbox or with unrestricted network access, the user currently has to approve that elevated access before the command runs.

**What Changed:** `chat.agent.sandbox.forceFirstExecutionInSandbox` is a preview setting, disabled by default. When enabled, a foreground `run_in_terminal` request that asks for elevated access first runs with the existing sandbox restrictions. The user is prompted to approve the requested elevated retry only after that sandboxed command exits with a nonzero exit code.

## Test Cases

For all test cases, use Agent mode with a functional terminal sandbox and configure:

```json
"chat.agent.sandbox.enabled": "on",
"chat.agent.sandbox.forceFirstExecutionInSandbox": true,
"chat.agent.sandbox.allowUnsandboxedCommands": true,
"chat.agent.sandbox.retryWithAllowNetworkRequests": true
```

### 1. Outside-sandbox request is deferred until the sandboxed run fails
- Ask the agent to use the terminal tool to write a uniquely named file under the user home directory (outside the workspace), and to request execution outside the sandbox if required.
- Verify that the command initially runs in the sandbox without showing an outside-sandbox confirmation.
- Verify that the sandboxed command fails because the target location is not writable from the sandbox.
- Verify that an **Allow** confirmation for the outside-sandbox retry appears only after that failed run. Approve it.
- Verify that the command reruns outside the sandbox and the file is created with the requested contents.

### 2. Unrestricted-network request is deferred until restricted networking fails
- Ask the agent to fetch `https://example.com` with the terminal tool and to request unrestricted network access while remaining sandboxed.
- Verify that the initial command runs with restricted sandbox networking, without an unrestricted-network confirmation.
- Verify that the initial request fails because network access is restricted.
- Verify that an **Allow** confirmation for the unrestricted-network retry appears only after that failure. Approve it.
- Verify that the command reruns successfully with network access while remaining sandboxed.

### 3. Default behavior remains unchanged when disabled
- Set `chat.agent.sandbox.forceFirstExecutionInSandbox` to `false`.
- Repeat either elevated-access scenario above.
- Verify that the elevated-access confirmation appears before any command is executed, rather than after a failed sandboxed attempt.
input.title: Test Plan: Run sandboxed before requesting terminal access elevation

input.body:
## Description

When using the **Copilot CLI agent inside the Agents window** (with a local git worktree open), the agent frequently gets stuck. Instead of reading files directly from my **local filesystem**, it repeatedly falls back to **GitHub commands/tools** (e.g. `get_file_contents`, code search against the remote repo) to read source files that already exist locally.

This causes the agent to:
- Loop for a long time fetching files remotely that are sitting right there on disk
- Burn through premium/usage cost ("dollars") without making progress
- Effectively stall for long stretches before producing any useful output

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open VS Code with a local git **worktree** of a repository.
2. Open the **Agents** window and start a **Copilot CLI** agent session.
3. Ask the agent to perform a task that requires reading several local files.
4. Observe the agent choosing GitHub remote file-reading tools instead of local file reads.

## Expected behavior

- The agent should **prefer local file reads** (the open worktree on disk) over GitHub remote fetches whenever the files are available locally.
- Remote GitHub tools should only be used when the content genuinely is not available locally.
- The agent should not get stuck in long loops re-fetching files remotely.

## Actual behavior

- The agent uses GitHub commands to read files that already exist in the local worktree.
- It gets stuck "analysing for hours," consuming usage/cost with no progress.

## Impact

- Wasted time (sessions stall for long periods).
- Wasted money ΓÇö usage is consumed without delivering results.

## Environment

- Surface: VS Code **Agents** window, **Copilot CLI** agent
- Working directory: local git **worktree**

## Additional notes

A clear local-first file-access policy (or stronger tool-selection guidance that deprioritizes remote GitHub reads when a local workspace/worktree is present) would likely resolve this.
input.title: Copilot CLI agent (Agents window) gets stuck reading files via GitHub MCP instead of the local worktree

input.title: Copilot completion feedback

input.body:
### Describe the bug

In voice mode, holding down the push-to-talk keybinding (`Ctrl+Shift+Space`) in the chat input behaves erratically instead of acting as push-to-talk.

**Expected:** Pressing and holding the keybinding should enter listening mode, and releasing the key should stop recording and send the request.

**Actual:** Holding the key rapidly toggles recording on and off ("weird things").

### Root cause

The chat-input voice actions (`agentsVoice.startVoiceInChat` and `agentsVoice.pttStopInChat`) called `voiceController.pttDown()` and `voiceController.pttUp()` synchronously, back-to-back:

```ts
voiceController.pttDown();
voiceController.pttUp();
```

Because `pttUp()` fires within ~0ms ΓÇö below the controller's tap threshold (`_PTT_TOGGLE_THRESHOLD_MS = 300`) ΓÇö the controller always interprets it as a *tap* and enters toggle mode, never true hold-to-talk. Worse, while the key is held, the OS emits keyboard auto-repeat `keydown` events, which re-dispatch the command repeatedly. Each repeat flips between `pttDown` (start listening), toggle, and finish/send, producing rapid start/stop cycling.

### Fix

Use `IKeybindingService.enableKeybindingHoldMode(commandId)` to implement real push-to-talk:

- On key press, call `pttDown()` (enter listening).
- The keybinding service swallows auto-repeat `keydown` events while hold mode is active, so the command is not re-dispatched mid-hold.
- When the key is released, the hold-mode promise resolves and we call `pttUp()` (stop + send).
- When invoked without a held key (toolbar button / command palette), `enableKeybindingHoldMode` returns `undefined` and we fall back to a single tap (toggle), preserving existing click behavior.

This mirrors the existing pattern in `voiceChatActions.ts` (`startVoiceChatWithHoldMode`).

### Area

`src/vs/workbench/contrib/agentsVoice/browser/agentsVoice.contribution.ts`
